  {
    "start_timestamp": "2026-05-14T04:04:31.630966Z",
    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-14T04:05:17.879909Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": " Good question, and one worth thinking carefully about before suggesting code. There are several distinct sub-questions hiding inside it:\n\n1. **How do you detect that you're on a low-tier or throttled device?**\n2. **How aggressively should you adapt, and when?**\n3. **What knobs are worth adapting?**\n4. **What's the failure mode if you guess wrong?**\n\nLet me think through each.\n\n## Device detection \u2014 what's actually available\n\nThe honest answer is: **detecting hardware tier from the browser is mostly bad and you should rely on it less than you'd think.**\n\nA few signals exist:\n\n- **`navigator.hardwareConcurrency`** \u2014 CPU thread count. Modern phones often report 6-8 (same as low-end laptops), so this is a weak signal. M-series Macs and budget Android both report 8.\n- **`navigator.deviceMemory`** \u2014 GB of RAM, rounded down. Reports `4` for any phone with 4-8GB. Available in Chrome and Edge but not Firefox or Safari.\n- **`matchMedia('(pointer: coarse)')`** \u2014 touch-primary device. Loosely correlates with mobile, but a tablet with a stylus has both fine and coarse pointers.\n- **`navigator.userAgent`** \u2014 string parsing. Brittle and increasingly meaningless as browsers reduce UA fingerprinting.\n- **`navigator.connection.effectiveType`** \u2014 4g/3g/2g/slow-2g. Signals network, not compute. A phone on wifi reports \"4g\" regardless of how slow the CPU is.\n- **`navigator.userAgentData.mobile`** \u2014 Chrome only. True on mobile.\n\nNone of these tell you \"this device is throttled right now.\" They tell you static facts about the device, not its current state.\n\n## The real signal: measure, don't predict\n\n**The thing that actually works is measuring frame time and adapting.** A high-end phone in a hot car is slower than a low-end phone in air conditioning; static signals miss that entirely. A user with a lot of other tabs open is slower than a clean profile. Thermal throttling, background sync, battery saver mode \u2014 none of these show up in device detection but all of them show up in frame time.\n\nThe lib *already* has the data: `wc.perfMetrics()` returns `framep50`, `framep95`, `framep99`. The right pattern is:\n\n1. **Run normally for the first few seconds.** Give the user the full experience.\n2. **Watch frame time.** If p95 stays under your budget, do nothing.\n3. **If p95 exceeds budget for a sustained window**, step down to a lighter configuration.\n4. **Stop adjusting once you find a stable point** (or you'll oscillate).\n\nThis is essentially how YouTube and Netflix adapt video quality \u2014 they measure throughput, they don't predict from hardware specs.\n\n## Static hints \u2014 when they DO help\n\nThere's one case where static hints earn their keep: **the very first frames**. Before you've measured anything, you have to guess at a starting configuration. For that:\n\n- `matchMedia('(pointer: coarse)')` is a reasonable proxy for \"start at a lower resolution\" \u2014 most coarse-pointer devices are mobile and benefit from a higher `SCALE`.\n- Beyond that, default high and adapt down.\n\nDon't try to detect \"low-tier mobile\" specifically and start it at the lowest setting. You'll be wrong often enough that high-end mobile users (which is most mobile users now) will get a worse experience than they should.\n\n## What knobs are worth adapting\n\nLooking at the lib, the cost-vs-quality knobs are roughly:\n\n| Knob | Cost reduction | Quality impact |\n|---|---|---|\n| `scale` (up) | Large \u2014 quadratic in grid cell count | Visible \u2014 pigment blocks get bigger |\n| `webgl(true)` | Already on by default when available | None |\n| `cursorPreview(false)` | Tiny | Loses brush-size visualization |\n| `advectionMode` | Modest | semi-Lagrangian \u2192 standard saves ~10% but reintroduces cross artifact |\n| `evaporation` (up) | Modest \u2014 fewer wet cells means fewer active cells | Wash dries faster, less time for bleed |\n| `edgeMode === 'closed'` | Modest \u2014 skips drainage pass | None functional, but loses edge effects |\n| `wetnessHeatmap(false)` | Modest \u2014 skips overlay render | None for users not using it |\n\nThe big one is `scale`. Doubling SCALE quarters the cell count. That's a massive win for compute-bound scenarios and the visual cost is usually acceptable (pigment looks slightly chunkier; most users won't notice).\n\n## A staged-adaptation strategy\n\nThe pattern I'd actually recommend:\n\n**Stage 0 \u2014 initial guess from static signals (one-time, before first frame)**\n\n```js\nconst isCoarsePointer = matchMedia('(pointer: coarse)').matches;\nconst isMobileUA = navigator.userAgentData?.mobile ?? false;\n\nconst initialScale = (isCoarsePointer || isMobileUA) ? 2.5 : 1.75;\n// Other defaults stay as-is. Don't disable features speculatively.\n```\n\nThis is the only static-signal use I'd defend. Mobile starts at a lower resolution; everyone else starts at the lib's default. No feature disabling at this stage.\n\n**Stage 1 \u2014 measure for 3 seconds at the initial config**\n\nDon't react instantly to spikes \u2014 give the JIT time to warm up, give the user a few interactions, and only react to *sustained* slowness.\n\n**Stage 2 \u2014 adapt only on clear, sustained signal**\n\n```js\n// After 3 seconds: check p95 frame time. If it's been bad for the\n// whole window, step down.\nsetInterval(() => {\n  const m = wc.perfMetrics();\n  if (m.framep95 > 33) {  // dropping below 30fps p95\n    // Step down ONE level at a time. Don't hop straight to lowest.\n    if (wc.scale() < 3) {\n      wc.scale(wc.scale() + 0.5);\n    } else if (wc.wetnessHeatmap()) {\n      wc.wetnessHeatmap(false);\n    } else if (wc.cursorPreview()) {\n      wc.cursorPreview(false);\n    }\n    // ... etc\n  }\n}, 5000);\n```\n\n**Stage 3 \u2014 STOP adapting once you find equilibrium**\n\nTrack how many adjustments you've made; cap it. Avoid the failure mode where one bad frame triggers a cascade of downgrades.\n\n## What I'd actually recommend you build\n\n**Don't auto-adapt by default.** I want to argue against the version of this question that's \"make the lib detect everything and adjust on its own.\" Here's why:\n\n- Users with throttled-but-recoverable conditions (a phone that's hot, a tab that just got CPU-starved by another tab) will get permanently degraded UX once the lib adapts down. There's no good way to detect recovery.\n- Auto-adaptation makes the lib's behavior unpredictable from the embedder's perspective. \"Why does it look different on this device?\" \"We adapted!\" \u2014 that's not a story most embedders want.\n- The lib is currently a deterministic tool. Adding adaptation makes it an opinionated tool. That's a real architectural shift, not just a feature.\n\n**Instead, make adaptation an embedder-driven opt-in.** Provide the data and the methods; let the embedder decide whether to use them.\n\nThree concrete things I'd add to the lib (none of them automatic):\n\n1. **A `getPerfTier()` method** that returns a hint based on `framep95` over the last few seconds. Returns `'good' | 'marginal' | 'poor'` or similar. The lib doesn't act on this; it just reports it.\n\n2. **An optional autoAdapt config**, off by default, that turns on the staged-adaptation logic above. Embedders who want it can opt in:\n   ```js\n   Washes.create(host, { autoAdapt: { targetFps: 30, maxScale: 3 } });\n   ```\n\n3. **A `quality` preset method** so embedders can implement their own adaptation cheaply:\n   ```js\n   wc.quality('high');    // scale=1.5, all features\n   wc.quality('medium');  // scale=2, all features\n   wc.quality('low');     // scale=3, heatmap off, cursor preview off\n   wc.quality('minimum'); // scale=4, donor-cell advection, ...\n   ```\n\nThe third one is the most useful in practice. Most embedders don't want to implement frame-time monitoring; they want a knob to expose to their users (\"Quality: High / Medium / Low\") and have it Just Work.\n\n## My actual answer to your question\n\nWhat I'd build, in order of cost-effectiveness:\n\n1. **First \u2014 and maybe only \u2014 `wc.quality(preset)`.** A simple method that bundles a coherent set of settings under named presets. This is something embedders can actually use, and it makes the lib's degradation modes explicit and discoverable. Maybe 30 lines of code.\n\n2. **Second \u2014 `Washes.create(host, { qualityHint: 'auto-mobile' })`** that uses just `matchMedia('(pointer: coarse)')` to pick a starting preset. Static-only, no runtime adaptation. Embedders who want \"good default for mobile\" get it. ~5 lines.\n\n3. **Third (only if you want it) \u2014 runtime auto-adapt.** This is real engineering with real failure modes. Not recommended unless you have a specific use case.\n\nIf you want to ship something now, I'd build #1 and #2 only. They're cheap, they're predictable, and they cover most of what \"auto-set for low-tier devices\" practically means without taking on the complexity of runtime adaptation.\n\nWant me to do that, or were you thinking of something different?",
    "citations": []
  }

    "text": " Good. Let me think about the gradient question before coding because it has a real \"how should this work\" decision in it.\n\n## Gradient background \u2014 what would it mean\n\nCurrently the paper is a single color (with subtle paper-texture variation overlaid). Adding a gradient requires deciding:\n\n1. **Where in the pipeline does the gradient apply?** Three options:\n   - **a)** The gradient *replaces* `paperColor` per-cell \u2014 so pigment composites over a per-cell paper color instead of a uniform one. K-M math sees the gradient as if it were the real paper.\n   - **b)** The gradient is rendered *behind* the canvas as a separate layer; pigment renders with transparency over it.\n   - **c)** Both \u2014 gradient affects K-M compositing AND shows through transparent areas.\n\n2. **What kind of gradient?** Linear (two stops, an angle), radial (center + edge colors), or a more complex multi-stop?\n\n3. **Does the gradient *react* to pigment?** I.e., do edge-darkening and granulation respect the gradient's value, or do they assume uniform paper?\n\nThe decision matters because they have very different implementations:\n\n- **Option (a) \u2014 replace paperColor per-cell** is the most physically correct. The lib already reads `paperColor` per-cell in some places (paperH); generalizing this to \"paper color is a function of grid position\" is doable but touches several K-M sites.\n- **Option (b) \u2014 gradient behind** is much cheaper. It's a CSS background on the canvas host, or a separate canvas underneath. Pigment renders with alpha and the gradient shows through where pigment is sparse.\n\n**Option (b) is what most people actually want.** It composes with everything (mask, fade, etc.), it's cheap, it's WYSIWYG with what designers expect from \"background gradient.\" The K-M math doesn't change \u2014 the gradient is purely a render-time layer.\n\n**Option (a) is what a physicist would build** but the results feel weird: a watercolor wash that \"knows\" about the gradient looks unnaturally tinted. Real watercolor on tinted paper does shift hue, but most users want the gradient as a *backdrop*, not as physical pigment-on-tinted-paper.\n\nI'd go with **(b)**. Cheap, predictable, matches expectations.\n\n## What I'll build for option (b)\n\nA `wc.background(spec)` API where spec is a gradient definition or a solid color. Internally it sets a CSS gradient on the host element behind the canvas. The painting canvas already supports transparency (the `transparent` toggle exposes this). Combine \"transparent canvas mode\" + \"gradient on host\" = pigment on gradient.\n\n**One subtlety:** the canvas currently renders with the paper color baked in. To see the gradient through pigment-less areas, the canvas needs alpha to be 0 in unpainted regions. The `transparent` toggle already does this. So `background(gradient)` should *also* turn on transparent mode automatically \u2014 or at least the docs should make this clear. I'll auto-enable it.\n\nDecisions for gradient API:\n\n- **Linear gradient with 2+ stops, plus angle.** Standard CSS syntax.\n- **Pass through to CSS directly** \u2014 accept any valid CSS background value, including `linear-gradient(...)`, `radial-gradient(...)`, solid colors, `url(...)`, etc. This makes the API extremely flexible at no implementation cost.\n- **API signature:** `wc.background(value)` \u2014 pass a CSS background string. Pass `null` or `''` to reset to default.\n\nNow let me also handle the original asks (#1 and #2 \u2014 quality presets and qualityHint).\n\n## Quality preset bundles\n\nWhat goes in each preset? Working backward from \"what costs what\":\n\n| Preset | scale | webgl | wetnessHeatmap | cursorPreview | advectionMode |\n|---|---|---|---|---|---|\n| `'high'` | 1.5 | true | (user-set) | true | semilag |\n| `'medium'` | 2.0 (default) | true | (user-set) | true | semilag |\n| `'low'` | 2.75 | true | force off | true | semilag |\n| `'minimum'` | 3.5 | true | force off | false | standard |\n\nA few judgment calls:\n- I'm NOT changing `wetnessHeatmap` setting at \"high\" or \"medium\" because it's a feature toggle, not a perf knob \u2014 user owns it.\n- I AM forcing it off at \"low\" and \"minimum\" because it does add full-canvas-pass overhead per frame.\n- `advectionMode: 'standard'` only at minimum because it brings back the cross artifact. Only worth it on truly struggling devices.\n\n## Plan, in build order\n\n1. Add `wc.background(value)` API \u2014 sets a CSS background on the host element, auto-enables `transparent` mode.\n2. Add `wc.quality(preset)` API \u2014 accepts `'high' | 'medium' | 'low' | 'minimum'`.\n3. Add `qualityHint` option to `create()` \u2014 accepts `'auto-mobile'` and applies a starting preset based on `matchMedia('(pointer: coarse)')`.\n4. UI: a Quality select dropdown in the Settings section. Background gradient: not exposing in the UI (designer-only API for now; would clutter the playground).\n5. Type declarations for new methods.\n6. Test.\n\nLet me build.\n\n### 1. background() API",

Bundles scale, advection mode, and a few\n  // feature toggles into named tiers. No runtime adaptation \u2014 this is\n  // a manual knob that embedders can expose to their users via\n  // \"Quality: High / Medium / Low\" UI, or call once at init based on\n  // their own heuristic.\n  //\n  // Trade-offs by preset:\n  //   'high'    \u2014 scale 1.5, fine grain. Best on desktop.\n  //   'medium'  \u2014 scale 2.0, library default. None of them change default\n          behavior.\n        </p>\n        <h3>Quality presets</h3>\n        <p>\n          New API <code>wc.quality(preset)</code> bundles\n          cost-vs-quality knobs into named tiers:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">'high'    \u2014 scale 1.5,  all features, fine grain (best on desktop)\n'medium'  \u2014 scale 2.0,  all features (library default)\n'low'     \u2014 scale 2.75, heatmap forced off (good on phones)\n'minimum' \u2014 scale 3.5,  donor-cell advection, no cursor preview, no heatmap\n            (last-resort fallback; brings back cardinal-cross artifact)</pre>\n        <p>\n          New \"Quality\" dropdown in Settings exposes this. Changing\n          any individual setting (Resolution, etc.) snaps the\n          dropdown to \"\u2014 Custom \u2014\" so the UI stays honest about\n          whether the current state matches a preset.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          The lib does NOT auto-adapt at runtime. This is a manual\n          knob that embedders can expose or call once at init based\n          on their own heuristic. Runtime adaptation is intentionally\n          left out: it has real failure modes (degrades permanently\n          on transient throttling, makes lib behavior unpredictable\n          from embedder POV).\n        </p>\n        <h3>qualityHint option</h3>\n        <p>\n          New create-time option:\n          <code>Washes.create(host, { qualityHint: 'auto-mobile' })</code>.\n          Uses <code>matchMedia('(pointer: coarse)')</code> to decide\n          whether to start at a lighter scale (2.5 for coarse-pointer\n          devices, default 1.75 otherwise). One-shot: only affects the\n          initial scale; doesn't enable runtime adaptation.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          Any explicit <code>scale</code> option wins over the hint.\n          Other quality knobs (advection, heatmap, cursor) aren't\n          touched by the hint &mdash; only scale is, since that's the\n          knob with the largest cost reduction and the smallest\n          visual cost.\n        </p>\n        <h3>Gradient backgrounds</h3>\n        <p>\n          New API <code>wc.background(value)</code> accepts any\n          valid CSS background value &mdash; solid color, linear\n          gradient, radial gradient, url(...), etc. Sets the CSS\n          background on the host element and auto-enables transparent\n          canvas mode so the background shows through pigment-less\n          areas. Pass <code>null</code> or <code>''</code> to clear.\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">wc.background('linear-gradient(180deg, #fef3c7, #ddd6fe)')\nwc.background('radial-gradient(circle at 30% 30%, #fef3c7, #f97316 80%)')\nwc.background('#1a1a1a')\nwc.background(null)</pre>\n        <p>\n          Pigment compositing (Kubelka&ndash;Munk) is unaffected.\n          The gradient is purely a render-layer backdrop, not a\n          physical paper tint &mdash; pigment still composites over\n          the lib's internal <code>paperColor</code>. The gradient\n          only shows where alpha is below ALPHA_FULL_AT (paper-thin\n          or unpainted areas).\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          Not exposed in the playground UI (would clutter for a\n          designer-only API), but works via the API directly.\n        </p>\n\n'''\nsrc = src.replace(old_cl, new_cl + old_cl)\nopen('/home/claude/watercolor.html', 'w').write(src)\nprint('docs updated')\nPY\n\nmv /home/claude/watercolor-v0.88-wiring.js /home/claude/watercolor-v0.89-wiring.js\nsed -i 's/v0\\.88-wiring/v0.89-wiring/g' /home/claude/watercolor-v0.89-wiring.js\n\ncd /home/claude && python3 << 'PY'\nsrc = open('/home/claude/watercolor.html').read()\nlines = src.splitlines()\ndef escape(js): return js.replace('</script>', '<\\\\/script>')\nlib = escape(open('/home/claude/watercolor-lib.js').read())\nwiring = escape(open('/home/claude/watercolor-v0.89-wiring.js').read())\nfor i, line in enumerate(lines):\n    if '<canvas id=\"canvas\"></canvas>' in line:\n        lines[i] = line.replace('<canvas id=\"canvas\"></canvas>', '<div id=\"canvas-host\"></div>')\n        break\nscript_start = None;     "text": " Two-part task. Let me think about each before coding.\n\n## Part 1 \u2014 Background UI\n\nLooking at what I have:\n- The lib's `wc.background(value)` accepts any CSS background string.\n- Bringing this to UI means deciding what controls to expose. Raw CSS string input is powerful but ugly; named gradient presets are limited but usable.\n\nThe right design choice is probably both. A small number of presets + a \"Custom...\" escape hatch that accepts a CSS string. But for the playground specifically, what designers actually want is interactive: pick gradient type (linear/radial/none), pick two colors, pick a direction. Let me keep it simple and useful:\n\n- **Toggle**: Solid / Linear / Radial / None\n- **Two color pickers** for the gradient stops\n- **Angle slider** for linear (0-360\u00b0)\n\nThat's three controls, all interactive. No CSS string entry needed. If a designer wants more (3-stop gradients, blur, image), they can drop to the API.\n\n## Part 2 \u2014 `onComplete` for splash and a no-animation option for traceSVG\n\nFor `splash.onComplete`:\n- Splash is synchronous-feeling but its pigment continues to evolve via the running sim. \"Complete\" is ambiguous. Two reasonable definitions:\n  - **a)** Stamp complete \u2014 the splash injection itself is done. (This is just after the function returns; basically synchronous.)\n  - **b)** Splash dissipated \u2014 pigment has settled, deluge wave has finished radiating, evaporation has caught up.\n\nDefinition **b** is more useful (matches how users think about deluges visually), but it's hard to define numerically \u2014 when is a splash \"done\"? I'd use a heuristic: when total active-cell count returns to within some threshold of its pre-splash level, fire the callback.\n\nActually, simpler and more useful: **fire onComplete after a configurable number of frames**, defaulting to something reasonable (maybe `duration` from caller, or fixed ~3 seconds). That's predictable and testable. Users can compose with their own timing if they want something different.\n\nEven simpler: just match how other API methods do it. `traceSVG` already has `onComplete` that fires when the path finishes. Splash should be analogous \u2014 fire when the stamping is mathematically complete. Since splash stamps in one frame, \"onComplete\" fires after one `requestAnimationFrame`. That's the cheapest, most predictable behavior.\n\nBut here's the question \u2014 **does the user mean a) \"splash has been injected\" or b) \"splash has visually dissipated\"?** I'll ask before assuming.\n\nFor `traceSVG` no-animation option:\n- Currently `traceSVG` draws each path stroke over time, animated.\n- Removing the animation means: drop all the pigment for all paths in a single frame, then return.\n- This is genuinely useful for offline-style rendering or for setting up a static composition. Easy to add \u2014 just iterate all paths and stamp them at once, then resolve the Promise.\n- API design: `traceSVG(svg, { animate: false })`, defaulting to true.\n\nLet me get clarification on the splash onComplete question before building, because b) is significantly more complex than a):\n\nFor the splash `onComplete`, two possible meanings:\n\n**a) \"Injection complete\"** \u2014 the stamp is on the canvas, the splash function has done its work. This is essentially synchronous \u2014 the callback fires after one frame. Useful for \"I want to splash and then immediately do X.\"\n\n**b) \"Splash has dissipated\"** \u2014 the deluge wave has finished radiating outward and the pigment has roughly settled into its final wet/dry state. Useful for \"I want to splash and wait until the visual has stabilized before doing X.\"\n\nOption **a** is trivial (3 lines, predictable). Option **b** requires defining \"dissipated\" numerically (probably \"active cell count has returned to baseline\" with a sensible timeout fallback). More complex but more useful for animation sequencing.\n\nWhich one do you want? Or both, with a flag (`onComplete` for a, `onSettled` for b)?\n\nAlso confirming on traceSVG \u2014 `{ animate: false }` should stamp all paths in a single frame and resolve immediately, right? Or do you want it to *render the final result* without showing the strokes (i.e., the SVG appears whole instantly, no per-stroke timing)?",

The animation IS the per-segment stamping over many frames.\n\nFor `animate: false`, the question is: do I stamp every segment of every path in a single synchronous loop (skipping the per-frame scheduling)?     "text": "I see the issue. **The `instant` loop has a bug for `durationMs`-based traces.** If the caller sets both `durationMs` and `instant: true`, the loop enters the time-based branch (lines 6491-6537), which uses `elapsed / durationMs` to compute the target index. Since the synchronous `while` loop runs essentially instantly (zero elapsed time), `easedT` stays at 0 and `targetIdx` stays at 0 \u2014 the loop never advances, and we'd infinite-loop on `st.idx < st.points.length` never becoming true.\n\nWait \u2014 the loop has `painted < cap` as a guard, and on the next iteration of the outer while, `st.idx` is still 0 but `st.points.length` is non-zero, so the outer loop re-enters and... actually, looking more carefully: on the first call, `targetIdx = 0` because `elapsed = 0`. The inner `while (st.idx < targetIdx && painted < cap)` immediately fails. The outer condition `svgTraceState && st.idx < st.points.length` is still true. The lib parses the SVG, decomposes it into\n * pen-stroke paths, and animates drawing them onto the canvas.\n */\nexport interface TraceSVGOptions {\n  /** Pigment to use for paths without a recognized fill/stroke color. */\n  pigment?: PigmentOption;\n  /** Display-pixel brush size while tracing. */\n  brushSize?: number;\n  /** 0..1 stamp strength per point. */\n  strength?: number;\n  /** Recognize chroma-key and pigment-hex colors as direct pigment assignments. Default true. */\n  triggerColors?: boolean;\n  /** Recognize any color and decompose into pigment mix via subtractive theory. Default false. */\n  approximateColor?: boolean;\n  /** Mirror the trace horizontally. */\n  flipX?: boolean;\n  /** Mirror the trace vertically. Default true (SVG y-axis is screen-down). */\n  flipY?: boolean;\n  /**\n   * If false, paint every point synchronously in one frame \u2014 no animation.\n   * The drawn SVG appears whole instantly. (v0.90)\n   *\n   * Equivalent to the older `instant: true` option (kept for backward compat).\n   * When false, any `durationMs` is ignored since there's nothing to animate.\n   */\n  animate?: boolean;\n  /** @deprecated Use `animate: false` instead. */\n  instant?: boolean;\n  /**\n   * Spread the trace over roughly this many ms with easing.\n   * Mutually exclusive with `animate: false` (the latter wins).\n   */\n  durationMs?: number;\n  /** Easing curve when durationMs is set. */\n  easing?: 'linear' | 'easeIn' | 'easeOut' | 'easeInOut' | 'penStroke';\n  /** Pause N ms between each top-level path. */\n  perStrokePauseMs?: number;\n  /** Optional bounds (in grid coordinates) for the SVG to fit inside. */\n  bounds?: { x: number; y: number; w: number; h: number };\n  /** Called once when the trace completes. Type dropdown + two color pickers +\n// angle slider (linear only). The wiring composes a CSS background\n// string from the controls and pushes it to wc.background(). All\n// four control rows are managed together: solid hides the angle and\n// the \"to\" color, linear shows everything, radial hides angle but\n// shows both colors. \"None\" clears everything and shows nothing.\nconst bgType        = document.getElementById('bg-type');\nconst bgColorsRow   = document.getElementById('bg-colors-control');\nconst bgAngleRow    = document.getElementById('bg-angle-control');\nconst bgColor1      = document.getElementById('bg-color-1');\nconst bgColor2      = document.getElementById('bg-color-2');\nconst bgColor1Label = document.getElementById('bg-color-1-label');\nconst bgColor2Wrap  = document.getElementById('bg-color-2-wrap');\nconst bgColorArrow  = document.getElementById('bg-color-arrow');\nconst bgAngle       = document.getElementById('bg-angle');\nconst bgAngleValue  = document.getElementById('bg-angle-value');\n\nfunction _buildBgCss() {\n  const type = bgType ? bgType.value : 'none';\n  if (type === 'none') return null;\n  const c1 = bgColor1 ? bgColor1.value : '#fef3c7';\n  const c2 = bgColor2 ? bgColor2.value : '#ddd6fe';\n  const ang = bgAngle ? +bgAngle.value : 180;\n  if (type === 'solid')   return c1;\n  if (type === 'linear')  return 'linear-gradient(' + ang + 'deg, ' + c1 + ', ' + c2 + ')';\n  if (type === 'radial')  return 'radial-gradient(circle at center, ' + c1 + ', ' + c2 + ')';\n  return null;\n}\n\nfunction _applyBgUI() {\n  const type = bgType ? bgType.value : 'none';\n  // Show/hide rows based on type.\n  if (bgColorsRow) bgColorsRow.style.display = (type === 'none') ? 'none' : 'flex';\n  if (bgAngleRow)  bgAngleRow.style.display  = (type === 'linear') ? 'flex' : 'none';\n  // For solid: hide the \"to\" color and the arrow, change label to just \"color\".\n  const isSolid = (type === 'solid');\n  if (bgColor2Wrap)  bgColor2Wrap.style.display  = isSolid ? 'none' : 'flex';\n  if (bgColorArrow)  bgColorArrow.style.display  = isSolid ? 'none' : 'inline';\n  if (bgColor1Label) bgColor1Label.textContent   = isSolid ? 'color' : 'from';\n  // Apply.\n  const css = _buildBgCss();\n  if (css === null) {\n    wc.background(null);\n  } else {\n    wc.background(css);\n  }\n}\n\nif (bgType) {\n  bgType.addEventListener('change', _applyBgUI);\n}\n[bgColor1, bgColor2, bgAngle].forEach(el => {\n  if (el) el.addEventListener('input', () => {\n    if (bgAngleValue && el === bgAngle) bgAngleValue.textContent = String(bgAngle.value);\n    _applyBgUI();\n  });\n});\n// Initial render value reflection (no CSS pushed; type defaults to 'none').\nif (bgAngleValue && bgAngle) bgAngleValue.textContent = String(bgAngle.value);",

When false, the lib stamps every point in\n          one synchronous frame; the drawn SVG appears whole the\n          instant the call returns. Any <code>durationMs</code>\n          becomes a no-op since there's no animation to spread.\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">wc.traceSVG(svg, { animate: false, pigment: 'rose' })\n// equivalent to:\nwc.traceSVG(svg, { instant: true, pigment: 'rose' })</pre>\n        <h3>Bug fix surfaced by this work</h3>\n        <p>\n          The original <code>instant: true</code> path would hang\n          if <code>durationMs</code> was also set. The synchronous\n          loop called <code>svgTraceStep()</code> repeatedly, but\n          <code>svgTraceStep</code> takes the time-based branch\n          when <code>durationMs &gt; 0</code> &mdash; that branch\n          computes <code>targetIdx = elapsed/durationMs</code>, and\n          in a synchronous loop, elapsed stays 0, so the loop never\n          advanced. Fixed by clearing <code>durationMs</code> on the\n          internal state object for the synchronous run + adding a\n          safety iteration counter that caps at\n          <code>points.length + 10</code>. Any existing code using\n          plain <code>instant: true</code> (without\n          <code>durationMs</code>) was unaffected by the bug or the\n          fix.\n        </p>\n        <h3>TypeScript declarations cleanup</h3>\n        <p>\n          <code>TraceSVGOptions</code> had two bogus fields\n          (<code>speed</code>, <code>translateX/Y</code>) and was\n          missing several real ones (<code>strength</code>,\n          <code>approximateColor</code>, <code>durationMs</code>,\n          <code>easing</code>, <code>perStrokePauseMs</code>,\n          <code>bounds</code>, <code>animate</code>,\n          <code>instant</code>). The return type was also wrong\n          (declared <code>Promise&lt;void&gt;</code> but the lib\n          returns a <code>number</code>). [
  {
    "start_timestamp": "2026-05-14T06:40:58.763339Z",
    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-14T06:41:45.109623Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": " Two good questions, very different problems. Let me think about each carefully.\n\n## #1 \u2014 Block interaction until FPS is good\n\nSome honest framing first: \"FPS is in a good state\" is a more slippery target than it sounds, and \"block until then\" is a UX pattern with real downsides.\n\n**The slippery part.** What counts as \"good\"? 60fps p99? 30fps p95? 60fps for 2 consecutive seconds? Each measurement choice has failure modes:\n\n- **Strict (60fps p99)**: lots of devices that would feel fine never qualify\n- **Generous (30fps p50)**: catches genuinely slow devices but lets through stuttery ones that frustrate users\n- **Sustained window (60fps for 3 seconds)**: most honest but creates a noticeable \"stuck\" period before the user can do anything\n\nThe lib already collects `framep50`, `framep95`, `framep99`, and `fps` via `perfMetrics()`. So you have the data \u2014 the question is when to release the gate.\n\n**The UX downside.** Four canvases idle-rendering-but-not-interactive is a confusing state. Users see motion and try to click on it; nothing happens; they get frustrated. The \"block until ready\" pattern works better when there's no motion at all during the block \u2014 a clean \"warming up...\" placeholder, then the canvases come alive.\n\nSo the better framing of your question is probably: **delay starting the simulation until the first canvas can sustain its frame budget, then start all four together.** Not \"render but block input\" \u2014 that's the worst of both worlds.\n\n### What I'd actually build\n\nTwo parts: a small **warm-up phase** that doesn't run the full simulation, and an **interaction gate** that releases once frame budget is met.\n\nThe lib doesn't currently have an \"off\" mode in the sense of \"create the canvas but don't simulate.\" It runs as soon as `create()` is called. To delay starting, you'd want a `wc.pause()` method (which connects naturally to your question #2). With that, the pattern becomes:\n\n```js\nconst canvases = [host1, host2, host3, host4].map(host =>\n  Washes.create(host, { paused: true })  // create, but don't animate yet\n);\n\n// Run one canvas at low intensity to probe device capability\nconst probe = canvases[0];\nprobe.resume();\nprobe.splash([{ x: 100, y: 100, velocity: 20 }], 'splash');\n\nlet readyAt = null;\nconst checkInterval = setInterval(() => {\n  const m = probe.perfMetrics();\n  if (m.framep95 < 17) {  // sustained 60fps p95\n    readyAt = readyAt || Date.now();\n    if (Date.now() - readyAt > 1500) {\n      // 1.5 seconds of sustained good fps \u2014 call it ready\n      clearInterval(checkInterval);\n      canvases.forEach(wc => wc.resume());\n      enableInteraction();  // your function: remove pointer-events:none from canvases, hide loader\n    }\n  } else {\n    readyAt = null;  // reset the sustained timer\n  }\n}, 200);\n\n// Fallback: don't block forever. After 5 seconds, release the gate\n// regardless and let the user proceed \u2014 even if perf isn't optimal,\n// \"stuck on a loading screen\" is worse than \"slightly janky.\"\nsetTimeout(() => {\n  clearInterval(checkInterval);\n  canvases.forEach(wc => wc.resume());\n  enableInteraction();\n}, 5000);\n```\n\n**The fallback timeout is the most important part of this.** Without it, a slow device gets a permanent block. With it, you guarantee the experience starts within 5 seconds even on the worst hardware.\n\n### What the lib would need to add\n\nFor this pattern to work, the lib needs three things:\n\n1. **`{ paused: true }` create option**: instantiate without starting the rAF loop. Currently `create()` always starts.\n2. **`wc.pause()` and `wc.resume()` methods**: stop and restart the rAF loop. This is also what answers your question #2.\n3. **A way to know perf metrics are valid**: `perfMetrics()` returns whatever the lib has measured. After only 3 frames, the p99 is meaningless. The lib should probably expose `m.sampleCount` or similar so you can wait for it to be statistically meaningful before judging.\n\n#1 and #2 are the same feature, basically. Let me think about #2 in more detail before building.\n\n## #2 \u2014 Pause and resume for SPA navigation\n\nThis is the right ask. A canvas off-screen shouldn't be running its physics simulation; it wastes CPU and battery. When the user navigates back to a screen that was previously visible, you want it to look the way they left it.\n\n**Three levels of \"pause\":**\n\n- **Level A \u2014 stop rendering**: don't call `render()` or `gpuRender()`. Cheap. Sim still runs in the background (wasted work).\n- **Level B \u2014 stop simulating**: don't call `simStep()`. Saves the bulk of the CPU work. Pigment state is frozen at the moment of pause.\n- **Level C \u2014 stop the rAF loop entirely**: no scheduling at all. Saves the small overhead of the rAF callback being called. Pigment state frozen.\n\nC is what you actually want for SPA navigation. The rAF loop should just stop until you call `resume()`.\n\n**What survives across pause/resume:**\n- All Float32Arrays (pigment, wetness, velocity, pressure, paper texture, mask)\n- All API state (current pigment, brush size, edge mode, etc.)\n- The canvas itself (DOM element with pixel data)\n\n**What needs handling:**\n- The `startTime` reference for animations (background washes, breathe mode, SVG traces). If 30 seconds passed during pause, you don't want the animation to \"jump ahead\" by 30 seconds; you want it to pick up where it left off. Means tracking `pausedAt` and adjusting timer references on resume.\n- Any pending splashes, deluges, or queued operations. These should pause too.\n- Pointer events should probably be ignored while paused (touching a paused canvas shouldn't queue a paint stroke that fires when resumed).\n\n**Browser-driven pausing.** The browser already automatically throttles rAF in hidden tabs \u2014 `document.visibilityState === 'hidden'` causes rAF to fire at ~1Hz or stop. So if your SPA simply removes the canvas from the DOM during navigation, the browser does some of this work for you. But:\n\n- DOM-removed elements: rAF still fires at full speed on canvases not in the DOM (browser doesn't know they're hidden)\n- `display: none` canvases: rAF still fires\n- Off-screen via CSS transform: rAF still fires\n\nSo you do need a real pause API; CSS hiding isn't enough.\n\n### What I'd build\n\nThe right scope is to add to the lib:\n\n1. **`wc.pause()` / `wc.resume()`** \u2014 stop and restart the rAF loop, with timer-reference adjustments so animations don't jump.\n2. **`wc.paused()`** \u2014 returns boolean. Useful for embedders to check state.\n3. **`{ paused: true }` create option** \u2014 instantiate paused. Useful for question #1.\n4. **Auto-pause on `document.visibilityState === 'hidden'`, opt-in** \u2014 optional flag that pauses when the tab hides, resumes when it shows. Off by default since some users want the sim to keep running in background tabs.\n\nThese are all small, well-contained changes. Together they answer both your questions.\n\n### A specific design question\n\nFor `pause()`, should pointer events keep working or should they be ignored too?\n\n- **If ignored**: the user touches a paused canvas, nothing happens. Resume + touch = paint stroke. Clean.\n- **If still active**: the user can paint on a paused canvas; their strokes accumulate in the simulation state, and when you resume, all the pigment they deposited starts evolving. Sort of magical, sort of confusing.\n\nI'd default to **ignored** (cleaner mental model) with the option to override:\n\n```js\nwc.pause();              // pointer events ignored, sim stopped\nwc.pause({ acceptInput: true });  // explicit override: accept input while paused\n```\n\nBut `acceptInput: true` is a weird thing to want. I'd ship the simpler version (always ignored when paused) and let someone ask for the override if they need it.\n\n### Auto-pause on tab-hidden \u2014 when this would surprise users\n\nThe opt-in `autoPauseOnHidden` flag is useful but worth flagging the failure mode: if the user navigates to another tab and back, they expect... what?\n\n- \"Pick up where I left off\" \u2014 auto-pause is right\n- \"Keep evolving while I was away\" \u2014 auto-pause is wrong\n\nThe right default depends on the use case. For your portfolio site where canvases are decoration, auto-pause is almost certainly right. For a long-running ambient simulation, keep evolving might be right. I'd make it opt-in with no default behavior change.\n\n## What I'd ship\n\nBoth items together as a single coherent release:\n\n1. `wc.pause()` and `wc.resume()` \u2014 stop/start the rAF loop, adjust timer references\n2. `wc.paused()` \u2014 returns boolean\n3. `{ paused: true }` and `{ autoPauseOnHidden: true }` create options\n4. Pointer events ignored while paused\n5. Updated TypeScript declarations\n\nThis answers question #2 directly and gives you the building blocks for question #1's pattern (which is embedder-level code, not lib code \u2014 I'd document it in the README rather than build it as an API).\n\nFor question #1 specifically, **I'd actively recommend against shipping the FPS-gating as a lib feature**. It's embedder policy, not lib mechanism. The lib should give you the perf data and the pause/resume primitives; how you use them to gate UI is your call. Hardcoding it in the lib would make it less flexible.\n\nWant me to build the pause/resume work? Or would you prefer to push on question #1 differently (e.g., make it a built-in feature anyway)?",
    "citations": []
  }
]

Let me think through what that means before coding.\n\n## What `pause({ acceptInput: true })` actually needs to do\n\nThe mental model: **freeze the simulation, but keep accepting pigment deposits.** When resumed, all the pigment the user deposited during pause becomes part of the simulation state and starts evolving.\n\nConcretely, what runs and what doesn't:\n\n| Operation | Paused (default) | Paused (acceptInput) | Running |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| rAF loop callback | stopped | stopped | running |\n| `simStep()` (physics) | skipped | skipped | runs |\n| `render()` | skipped | runs on input | runs |\n| Pointer events | ignored | accepted | accepted |\n| `paintAt`, `splash`, `traceSVG` | queued? error? | runs | runs |\n\nThe interesting questions:\n\n1. **When the user paints during `acceptInput` pause, does it render immediately?** Yes \u2014 otherwise they don't see their strokes. Each `paintAt` triggers a one-shot `render()` so the user sees their deposits, but no `simStep()` happens between them. The deposits sit there inert until resume.\n\n2. **What about programmatic calls (`splash`, `traceSVG`) during pause?** Same logic. They deposit, they trigger a render, sim is frozen.\n\n3. **What about `traceSVG` with `animate: true`?** That has its own RAF loop independent of the sim loop. The user can call `traceSVG` while paused, and... what? My instinct: traceSVG should still animate (it's a render-time animation, not a sim-time one), and each painted point is just a deposit. The sim stays frozen.\n\n4. **What about `obliterate()`?** It modifies pigment over time. If sim is paused, should obliterate still run? It's a time-based animation, so probably yes \u2014 same logic as traceSVG. Animation runs, sim doesn't.\n\n5. **What about animations like `setAnimation('breathe')`?** These modulate the sim's parameters over time. They should probably pause too \u2014 they're part of the sim loop semantics.\n\nSo the rule of thumb is: **pause stops the simulation step, but render and animation loops continue.** Input adds pigment but doesn't trigger sim. Resume restarts the sim step.\n\nThis matches your \"paint into a paused canvas, then resume\" use case cleanly.\n\n## What the lib looks like now\n\nLet me look at the current loop:",

    "type": "text",
    "text": "Looking at the loop, the things I'd want to skip when paused:\n\n- `applyPendingPaint` \u2014 no, the user is painting WITH `acceptInput: true`, so pending paint must still apply\n- `animationStep` (background animations like breathe, drift) \u2014 skip if paused\n- `visualizationStep` \u2014 skip if paused\n- `svgTraceStep` \u2014 keep running (animations during pause is desired per my reasoning)\n- `simStep` \u2014 skip if paused (this is THE pause behavior)\n- `render` \u2014 needs nuance: skip the per-frame render, but allow it to fire when input arrives\n- `timeWashStep` \u2014 skip if paused (time-driven background)\n- `fadeStep` \u2014 skip if paused (would cause pigment to slowly disappear on a paused canvas)\n\nBut here's a subtlety: **if the user is painting during pause, `applyPendingPaint` deposits new pigment. We need to render that \u2014 otherwise the user paints into the void.** So during pause-with-input, `render()` should still run when there's actually been a deposit.\n\nThe cleanest design: keep the loop running at full rAF rate, but skip `simStep`, `fadeStep`, `timeWashStep`, `animationStep`, `visualizationStep`. Always run `applyPendingPaint` and the conditional render. That way:\n\n- Idle pause: nothing happens, but rAF still ticks (cheap \u2014 empty loop is microseconds)\n- Active-input pause: paint deposits, renders, but no physics\n\nActually, on second thought \u2014 running an empty rAF loop at 60Hz still wastes ~60 callbacks/second of CPU on each of 4 canvases. That's 240 callbacks/sec doing essentially nothing. Better design: **completely stop rAF when paused, restart it when pointer events arrive.** That way truly-idle pause is zero cost.\n\nWait, but then when the user releases pointer mid-stroke, how does the lib know to keep running render for one more frame?     "type": "text",
    "text": "OK, so the architecture is: pointer events set `_pendingPaintX/Y`, then `applyPendingPaint()` is called each frame to commit the pending position into actual `paintAt` calls. This relies on the per-frame loop.\n\nFor the pause-with-input case, I have two choices:\n\n**Option A \u2014 keep rAF running, but skip sim/fade/animations.** Always-on full-rate rAF loop, just with most work skipped. Simple to implement. Wastes ~60 calls/sec when truly idle.\n\n**Option B \u2014 stop rAF, restart it on pointer event, stop again when stroke ends.** More efficient but has edge cases (when does it stop? After how many idle frames? What if the user pauses without input and the loop never starts?).\n\nOption A is simpler and the overhead is genuinely small (a few microseconds per frame doing essentially nothing). For 4 canvases that's <1ms of CPU per second total. I'll go with A.\n\nSo the actual design:\n\n- `wc.pause()` and `wc.pause(opts)` \u2014 both accepted. `opts` is `{ acceptInput?: boolean }`, defaulting to `false`.\n- When paused without `acceptInput`: pointer events ignored (pointer-events: none on the canvas), no sim, no fade, no animations. rAF loop runs but skips everything.\n- When paused with `acceptInput`: pointer events accepted, paint deposits and renders, no sim, no fade, no animations. rAF loop runs.\n- `wc.resume()` \u2014 restart sim. Adjust startTime references for any time-based animations so they don't jump.\n- `wc.paused()` \u2014 returns boolean (or maybe `{ paused: true, acceptInput: true }`?).\n\nI'll keep `paused()` returning a simple boolean and add a separate `paused.acceptInput` getter if needed. Actually simpler: `wc.paused()` returns `false | 'frozen' | 'paint-only'`. Three-state return.\n\nHmm, that might be clever for clever's sake. Let me make it: `wc.paused()` returns the options object `{ paused, acceptInput }`, or just `false` when not paused. Actually plain booleans are easier to consume.\n\nOK final API design:\n\n```js\nwc.pause();                            // freeze sim, ignore input\nwc.pause({ acceptInput: true });       // freeze sim, accept input\nwc.resume();                           // restart sim\nwc.paused();                           // \u2192 boolean\nwc.paused() && wc.pauseOptions();      // \u2192 { acceptInput: boolean }  (or undefined if not paused)\n```\n\nActually that's two methods. Simpler:\n\n```js\nwc.pause(opts?);   // opts = { acceptInput?: boolean }\nwc.resume();\nwc.paused();       // returns false | { acceptInput: boolean }\n```\n\nFalsy when running, object when paused (with acceptInput state). Compact and unambiguous.\n\nLet me build it.",

When off the calls\n  // compile down to a single boolean check at each gate \u2014 negligible.\n  const t0 = perfEnabled ? performance.now() : 0;\n\n  // v0.90 \u2014 applyPendingPaint runs whether or not we're paused. Without acceptInput, pointer events have\n  // been short-circuited at the event listener so _pendingPaintX is\n  // never set and this is a no-op anyway.\n  applyPendingPaint && applyPendingPaint();\n  // Animation/visualization/wash steps mutate sim parameters or\n  // pigment over time. Without\n  // framesSinceIdleCheck++ the idle-skip check at shouldRunSim() is\n  // permanently stuck below IDLE_CHECK_INTERVAL and the sim never\n  // enters its idle path \u2014 every frame runs simStep + render even\n  // when the canvas is completely settled. (This was a real bug in\n  // an earlier draft of the lib.)\n  framesSincePaint++;\n  framesSinceIdleCheck++;\n  framesSinceFade++;\n\n  const tBeforeSim = perfEnabled ? performance.now() : 0;\n  // v0.90 \u2014 pause gates the physics step. But fadeStep\n  // modifies d[] regardless, so without a forced render, the visible\n  // pigment doesn't update on a dry canvas. If the user paints during pause, `markCanvasActive()` sets it to false, render runs.       "new_str": "    _qualityCurrent = preset;\n    return preset;\n  },\n\n  // v0.90 \u2014 Pause the simulation. Stops simStep, fadeStep, time-wash,\n  // and animation steps. The rAF loop continues so input can still\n  // produce renders (when acceptInput is true) and so resume() takes\n  // effect immediately on the next frame.\n  //\n  //   wc.pause()                            \u2014 freeze sim, ignore input\n  //   wc.pause({ acceptInput: true })       \u2014 freeze sim, accept paint input\n  //\n  // While paused-with-acceptInput, the user (or programmatic code via\n  // splash/paintAt/traceSVG) can deposit new pigment. Each deposit\n  // triggers a one-shot render so the user sees their stroke\n  // immediately. The deposits sit inert until resume() \u2014 they don't\n  // bleed, flow, or evaporate while paused.\n  //\n  // Pointer events on the canvas are ignored when paused without\n  // acceptInput. The canvas's CSS pointer-events is unchanged\n  // (parent overlays and other listeners still work normally); only\n  // the lib's own pointer handlers short-circuit.\n  pause(opts) {\n    opts = opts || {};\n    if (_paused) {\n      // Already paused \u2014 just update acceptInput if it changed.\n      _pauseAcceptInput = !!opts.acceptInput;\n      return api;\n    }\n    _paused = true;\n    _pauseAcceptInput = !!opts.acceptInput;\n    _pauseStartedAt = (typeof performance !== 'undefined' ? performance.now() : Date.now());\n    // If a stroke was in progress when pause was called and we're\n    // not accepting input, end it cleanly so the user can't return\n    // to a half-state on resume.\n    if (!_pauseAcceptInput) {\n      _isPainting = false;\n      _activePointerId = null;\n      _pendingPaintX = null;\n      _pendingPaintY = null;\n    }\n    return api;\n  },\n\n  // v0.90 \u2014 Resume from pause. Adjusts wall-clock timer references\n  // for any animations that were mid-flight at pause time, so they\n  // pick up where they left off instead of jumping ahead by the\n  // pause duration.\n  resume() {\n    if (!_paused) return api;\n    const now = (typeof performance !== 'undefined' ? performance.now() : Date.now());\n    const pauseDuration = now - _pauseStartedAt;\n    // SVG trace state has a wall-clock startTime; shift it forward\n    // by the pause duration so easing continues from where it was.\n    if (typeof svgTraceState !== 'undefined' && svgTraceState && svgTraceState.startTime) {\n      svgTraceState.startTime += pauseDuration;\n    }\n    _paused = false;\n    _pauseAcceptInput = false;\n    _pauseDirty = false;\n    // The sim has been frozen for an arbitrary amount of time; cells\n    // may have been marked dirty by deposits during pause. Force a\n    // wake so the sim restarts processing them.\n    markCanvasActive();\n    return api;\n  },\n\n  // v0.90 \u2014 Pause state inspector. Returns false when running,\n  // or an object { acceptInput: boolean } when paused. Using a\n  // falsy-or-object return lets callers do both\n  // `if (wc.paused())` and `wc.paused()?.acceptInput`.\n  paused() {\n    if (!_paused) return false;\n    return { acceptInput: _pauseAcceptInput };\n  },\n\n",

Use case: pause canvases\n          when their containing component unmounts (SPA navigation),\n          resume when it re-mounts. State is preserved across pause\n          (pigment, wetness, velocity, pressure all frozen at the\n          moment of pause) so resume picks up exactly where it left\n          off.\n        </p>\n        <h3>API</h3>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">wc.pause()                          // freeze sim, ignore input\nwc.pause({ acceptInput: true })     // freeze sim, accept paint input\nwc.resume()                         // restart sim, adjust animation timers\nwc.paused()                         // false | { acceptInput: boolean }</pre>\n        <h3>What pauses</h3>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><code>simStep</code> (advection, pressure, diffusion, edge effects)</li>\n          <li><code>fadeStep</code> (pigment fade \u2014 wouldn't make sense to slowly disappear from a paused canvas)</li>\n          <li><code>timeWashStep</code> (background washes)</li>\n          <li><code>animationStep</code> (breathe, drift, pulse)</li>\n          <li><code>visualizationStep</code></li>\n        </ul>\n        <h3>What still runs</h3>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><code>applyPendingPaint</code> (commits pointer position to grid)</li>\n          <li><code>svgTraceStep</code> (path-time animations stamp pigment but don't evolve it)</li>\n          <li><code>render</code> (one-shot when there's a deposit; see <code>_pauseDirty</code> flag)</li>\n          <li>The rAF loop itself (so resume takes effect on the next frame)</li>\n        </ul>\n        <h3>acceptInput: true</h3>\n        <p>\n          Programmatic deposits (<code>splash</code>, <code>paintAt</code>,\n          <code>traceSVG</code>) always work during pause &mdash; the\n          pigment deposits visibly via a one-shot render and sits\n          inert until resume(). With <code>acceptInput: true</code>,\n          pointer input does the same thing: the user can paint on a\n          paused canvas, see their strokes, and have them all start\n          evolving the moment <code>resume()</code> is called. Useful\n          for \"compose a layout, then unfreeze and let it bleed\"\n          workflows.\n        </p>\n        <h3>Resume timer-shift</h3>\n        <p>\n          Time-based animations with wall-clock <code>startTime</code>\n          references (currently just SVG traces with\n          <code>durationMs</code>) are shifted forward by the elapsed\n          pause duration on resume. The trace picks up at the same\n          point in its easing curve instead of jumping ahead by the\n          pause length.\n        </p>\n        <h3>What pause does NOT do</h3>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li>Does not stop the rAF loop (so resume is instant). Cost during pause: a handful of microseconds per frame for the early-return checks.</li>\n          <li>Does not remove pointer event listeners. The lib's handlers short-circuit on <code>_paused &amp;&amp; !_pauseAcceptInput</code>, but any other event listeners on the canvas (your own, browser default scroll handling, etc.) continue normally.</li>\n          <li>Does not change CSS. If you want the canvas to look visually paused, dim it or add a <code>pointer-events: none</code> via your own CSS.</li>\n          <li>Does not auto-pause on tab hidden. The browser already throttles rAF in hidden tabs; if you want explicit pause on visibility change, listen to <code>document.visibilitychange</code> and call pause/resume yourself.</li>\n        </ul>\n        <p>\n          Not exposed in the playground UI (no useful interactive\n          test &mdash; pause is for embedders, not for casual playing\n          with the lib). API only.\n        </p>\n\n'''\nsrc = src.replace(old_cl, new_cl + old_cl)\nopen('/home/claude/watercolor.html', 'w').write(src)\nprint('docs updated')\nPY\n\nmv /home/claude/watercolor-v0.90-wiring.js /home/claude/watercolor-v0.91-wiring.js\nsed -i 's/v0\\.90-wiring/v0.91-wiring/g' /home/claude/watercolor-v0.91-wiring.js\n\ncd /home/claude && python3 << 'PY'\nsrc = open('/home/claude/watercolor.html').read()\nlines = src.splitlines()\ndef escape(js): return js.replace('</script>', '<\\\\/script>')\nlib = escape(open('/home/claude/watercolor-lib.js').read())\nwiring = escape(open('/home/claude/watercolor-v0.91-wiring.js').read())\nfor i, line in enumerate(lines):\n    if '<canvas id=\"canvas\"></canvas>' in line:\n        lines[i] = line.replace('<canvas id=\"canvas\"></canvas>', '<div id=\"canvas-host\"></div>')\n        break\nscript_start = None;     "type": "text",
    "text": "Shipped v0.91 with pause/resume.\n\n**API:**\n```js\nwc.pause()                         // freeze, ignore input\nwc.pause({ acceptInput: true })    // freeze, accept paint input\nwc.resume()                        // restart sim, adjust animation timers\nwc.paused()                        // false | { acceptInput: boolean }\n```\n\n**Key design decisions:**\n\n- **Loop keeps running during pause.** The rAF callback fires every frame, just with most work skipped. Costs ~microseconds per frame per canvas, so 4 canvases costs <1ms/sec of CPU when paused. The alternative \u2014 stopping rAF entirely \u2014 has uglier edge cases around when to restart it.\n\n- **Programmatic deposits always work during pause.** `splash`, `paintAt`, `traceSVG` all still deposit pigment when paused. The pigment is visible immediately (via a one-shot render gated by `_pauseDirty`) but doesn't evolve until resume. This makes `pause({ acceptInput: false })` still useful \u2014 you can compose a layout programmatically on a frozen canvas.\n\n- **`acceptInput: true` lets pointer input through too.** This is your use case: paint on a frozen canvas, see your strokes appear, then call `resume()` to let the whole composition start bleeding. Should feel like building up a wet sketch and then \"starting time.\"\n\n- **Resume shifts animation timers.** SVG traces in progress at pause time have their `startTime` shifted forward by the pause duration so they pick up at the same point in the easing curve. Without this, a 30-second pause during a 2-second trace would resume with the trace already \"done.\"\n\n**For your SPA navigation use case** (question #2), the pattern is:\n\n```js\n// Component mount\nconst wc = Washes.create(host);\n\n// User navigates away \u2014 your route change handler:\nwc.pause();\n\n// User navigates back:\nwc.resume();\n// The canvas looks exactly like the user left it. Pigment that was\n// bleeding when they left starts bleeding again from where it was.\n```\n\n**For your 4-canvases case**, you can now use this as the building block for the FPS-gating pattern from question #1 (start canvases paused, probe with one, release them all together).\n\nA few things I deliberately did NOT do, worth flagging:\n\n- **No `{ paused: true }` create option.** Not needed \u2014 you can call `pause()` immediately after `create()`. The lib's `create()` is synchronous so there's no race condition. Adding it as an option felt like API surface for the sake of it.\n\n- **No auto-pause on tab hidden.** The browser already throttles rAF in hidden tabs (~1Hz or stopped), so explicit auto-pause has minimal benefit and surprises users who want background sims to keep running. If you want it, listen for `document.visibilitychange` and call pause/resume yourself.\n\n- **No CSS dimming when paused.** That's a visual UX choice you should control, not the lib. Apply `opacity: 0.5` or whatever feels right from your CSS.\n\nReady for question #1 whenever you are.",
    "citations": []
  }

Let me think through each before writing anything, because two of these are conceptual and need conversation before code.\n\n## What's straightforward (will build)\n\n1. **Pause UI** \u2014 toggle button + acceptInput checkbox in Settings.\n2. **Evaporation slider** \u2014 already a `wc.evaporation()` API, just needs UI.\n3. **TypeScript min/max** \u2014 JSDoc tags on numeric parameters in `.d.ts`. TypeScript itself doesn't enforce ranges (it has no integer subrange types), but `@min`/`@max` JSDoc tags are picked up by IntelliSense, ESLint rules, and runtime validators like Zod. Best-effort, but worth doing.\n4. **Increase water load max** \u2014 find the current cap, raise it, check for downstream issues.\n\n## What needs conversation before code\n\n**5. Water \"thickness\" / pooling / rolling down during pause+gravity.** This is a conceptually new feature, not just an exposed API. Let me think about it honestly.\n\nThe current model already has a `wet[]` array that *is* a thickness in a sense \u2014 it's the per-cell water amount. And the velocity field `(u, v)` already responds to gravity (when `closed-gravity` mode is on). And the advection step moves wet AND pigment together. So in principle, the request is satisfied by combining what's already there: pause the simulation but keep the velocity update running.\n\nBut pause currently stops *everything* sim-related. So the literal \"pools up, rolls down\" behavior with gravity active during pause requires either:\n- A new pause sub-mode like `pause({ physicsOnly: true })` that keeps velocity+advection but stops other sim work, OR\n- Making the user resume (which kicks the full sim) and accept that's what \"rolling down\" looks like\n\nThe honest question is: **what visual is the user actually after?** I see two distinct interpretations:\n\n- **Interpretation A**: \"While I'm painting with the canvas paused, I want droplets I paint to slowly slide down toward the bottom edge, like wet paint on real watercolor paper.\" This is a continuous physics-but-paused-otherwise behavior.\n\n- **Interpretation B**: \"While paused with acceptInput, I want my deposits to *accumulate* (no diffusion or evaporation), so they stay sharp and editable. When I resume, gravity takes over and they bleed/roll.\" This is the existing pause-with-acceptInput model, with `closed-gravity` after resume providing the \"rolling\" effect.\n\nBoth are reasonable. **B is already supported with what we built**. A is a new feature that requires partial-pause semantics.\n\nIf A is what you want, the right shape is probably a third pause flag: `wc.pause({ acceptInput: true, physics: 'gravity-only' })` or similar \u2014 keeps the velocity update + advection running so gravity still affects pigment, but skips diffusion/edge/evaporation work.\n\nNeed to ask which one you want before building. Worth noting that A is more interesting visually but more complex; B is \"free\" \u2014 you already have it.\n\n**6. Pigment composited on top of the gradient.** This is the option (a) I described back when we built `background()` \u2014 replace `paperColor` per-cell with values sampled from the gradient. Pigment then composites via Kubelka-Munk against the gradient as if it were physical paper.\n\nI argued against this at the time, calling it \"what a physicist would build\" but \"looks weird\" because watercolor on tinted paper does shift the pigment hue in ways most users find surprising. I still believe that \u2014 but if you specifically want the K-M-on-gradient behavior, it's buildable.\n\nTwo challenges:\n\n- **Sampling the gradient per-cell.** The current `background()` accepts arbitrary CSS \u2014 `linear-gradient(...)`, `radial-gradient(...)`, `url(...)`, etc. To sample those per-cell, the lib would need to parse the CSS itself, which is a pile of work for limited gain. Or the lib needs a *separate* API that accepts a structured gradient definition (`{ type: 'linear', stops: [...], angle: X }`) and samples internally.\n- **WebGL shader compatibility.** Currently the shader uses a single `uPaper` uniform. To support per-cell paper, the shader needs to either: receive the gradient as a uniform definition (then compute it per-pixel) OR receive a texture sampled per-cell. Either is doable, both add shader complexity.\n\nThe cleanest design would be a new `wc.paperGradient(spec)` API that takes a structured definition and applies it as the per-cell paper color. The existing `wc.background()` keeps doing what it does (CSS-only, decorative backdrop, no K-M effect).\n\nThat makes 6 a substantial feature \u2014 comparable in scope to the v0.81-0.84 boundary work. Worth doing if you specifically want the visual, but I'd want to confirm before building.\n\n## Plan\n\nLet me ship 1, 2, 3, 4 now (low risk, well-bounded), and ask about 5 and 6.\n\nFor **5**, my question is: **interpretation A (live gravity-driven rolling during pause) or interpretation B (your existing pause-with-acceptInput, with gravity kicking in on resume)?**\n\nFor **6**, my question is: **is the look you want where the painting \"tints\" the gradient (KM on gradient), or did you mean something simpler \u2014 like the gradient just showing through the painting more cleanly?** I want to make sure we're building the right thing because option-A K-M-on-gradient is real work and may not give you what you imagined.\n\nLet me build 1-4 while you think about 5 and 6.",

The acceptInput\n// checkbox is enabled only when the pause toggle is on (it has no\n// meaning when running). Re-clicking pause while acceptInput is\n// checked re-applies the pause with the current checkbox state, so\n// flipping acceptInput mid-pause works without forcing the user to\n// resume and re-pause.\nconst pauseBtn = document.getElementById('toggle-pause');\nconst pauseAcceptCheckbox = document.getElementById('pause-accept-input');\nconst pauseAcceptLabel = document.getElementById('pause-accept-input-label');\nfunction _applyPauseUI() {\n  const paused = !!wc.paused();\n  if (pauseBtn) {\n    pauseBtn.classList.toggle('on', paused);\n    pauseBtn.setAttribute('aria-pressed', String(paused));\n    pauseBtn.textContent = paused ? 'Resume' : 'Pause';\n    // Re-add the info marker (textContent overwrites it).\n    const info = document.createElement('span');\n    info.className = 'info-marker';\n    info.setAttribute('aria-hidden', 'true');\n    info.textContent = '\u24d8';\n    pauseBtn.appendChild(info);\n  }\n  if (pauseAcceptCheckbox) pauseAcceptCheckbox.disabled = !paused;\n  if (pauseAcceptLabel) pauseAcceptLabel.style.opacity = paused ? '1' : '0.5';\n}\nif (pauseBtn) {\n  pauseBtn.addEventListener('click', () => {\n    if (wc.paused()) {\n      wc.resume();\n    } else {\n      wc.pause({ acceptInput: !!(pauseAcceptCheckbox && pauseAcceptCheckbox.checked) });\n    }\n    _applyPauseUI();\n  });\n}\nif (pauseAcceptCheckbox) {\n  pauseAcceptCheckbox.addEventListener('change', () => {\n    // Only re-pause if currently paused.     "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "Shipped v0.92 with items 1-4. Now the two open questions I want to talk through before writing code.\n\n## #5 \u2014 Water \"thickness\" / rolling pools\n\nTwo interpretations of what you want, with very different implementations:\n\n**Interpretation A \u2014 Live gravity-driven rolling during pause.** While paused with acceptInput, droplets you paint slide down the page over time, like wet paint on tilted real paper. Sim is \"paused\" in the sense that diffusion/evaporation/edge effects don't run, but velocity+advection DO run (so gravity moves stuff). This is a new pause sub-mode.\n\n**Interpretation B \u2014 Pool while paused, roll on resume.** Your current setup almost does this. With `pause({ acceptInput: true })` + `closed-gravity` mode + downward gravity: paint stays sharp while paused, then bleeds and rolls down when you call `resume()`. The \"thickness\" is the wet[] value accumulating from your paint strokes; on resume, gravity advects it.\n\nIf you want **A**, it's a real feature: I'd add `pause({ acceptInput: true, gravity: true })` that keeps the velocity+advection pipeline alive but skips diffusion/evaporation/edge work. Maybe 1-2 hours of work and some testing of which sim phases to keep vs skip. I should also note that A makes the visual *behavior* during pause noticeably more complex \u2014 the user is no longer painting onto a static canvas, they're painting onto a slowly-flowing-down canvas. Could feel magical, could feel annoying depending on use case.\n\nIf you want **B**, no work needed \u2014 already supported with the existing API. Set `edgeMode('closed-gravity')`, `gravityDirection('down')`, `gravityStrength(0.05)`, `pause({ acceptInput: true })`, paint, then `resume()`.\n\nWhich one matches what you imagined?\n\n## #6 \u2014 Pigment composited on top of gradient\n\nI want to make sure we're on the same page about what this means before building, because I argued against it last time and want to push back gently.\n\n**What \"pigment composites on top of gradient\" technically means:** the gradient color is treated as the *physical paper color* for Kubelka-Munk math. Right now the lib does `kmReflect(K, S, thickness, paperColor)` where `paperColor` is a single uniform color. Changing this means `paperColor` varies per-cell, sampled from a gradient.\n\n**The visual you'd actually get:** pigment over a yellow region of the gradient becomes greenish (yellow paper \u00d7 any-pigment via K-M produces a yellow shift). Pigment over a purple region becomes more saturated. Pigment over black regions becomes very saturated (almost no light returns). The painting *tints* with the gradient in a way that follows watercolor optics.\n\n**This is real, physically correct, and often surprising.** Real watercolor on tinted paper does look like this. But most people who say \"I want the painting on a gradient\" actually mean **the gradient should show through unpainted areas, and pigment should look like pigment**, not like pigment-tinted-by-gradient. The current `wc.background()` already does that simpler thing.\n\nTwo questions to settle:\n\n1. **What's the visual you imagined?** Pigment that shifts color based on the gradient underneath (the physically correct K-M result), OR pigment that stays its own color with the gradient just showing through in unpainted regions (what we have now)?\n\n2. **If you want the K-M-on-gradient behavior**, are you OK with the gradient API being more restricted? CSS `linear-gradient()` strings are parsed by the browser; I can't sample them per-cell without re-implementing the CSS gradient parser. I'd need a separate structured API: `wc.paperGradient({ type: 'linear', angle: 180, stops: [['#abc', 0], ['#def', 1]] })` or similar.\n\nThe simplest path forward: if you want #6's K-M-on-gradient behavior, I'd add `wc.paperGradient(spec)` with a structured definition. It composes orthogonally with `wc.background(cssString)` (which keeps doing what it does \u2014 decorative backdrop). You could use both: K-M shifts the pigment with `paperGradient`, AND the CSS gradient shows through unpainted areas with `background`. Or you could use just one.\n\nWant me to build #6 as `wc.paperGradient()`? And which interpretation of #5 do you want?",
    "citations": []
  }

    "text": " Good question \u2014 and worth thinking about what \"inward radial\" actually means physically before coding, because it has stability considerations that \"outward radial\" doesn't.\n\n## The math difference\n\n**Outward radial** (current behavior): each cell's bias points away from canvas center. The center cell has zero bias; cells far from center get the strongest outward pull. As pigment moves outward, cells get more bias in the outward direction \u2014 this is **divergent**: pigment leaves any given region faster than it arrives. The natural endpoint is \"everything at the edges.\"\n\n**Inward radial** (proposed): each cell's bias points *toward* the canvas center. Cells far from center pull strongest toward center. As pigment moves inward, cells slow their bias (since they're closer to center). The natural endpoint is \"everything piles up at the center.\"\n\nThat sounds symmetric, but it's not. Inward radial has a **singularity at the origin**: as pigment converges toward (cx, cy), the bias magnitude doesn't drop to zero smoothly \u2014 the *direction* becomes undefined when you're exactly at the center. Math-wise, the vector `(cx-x, cy-y) / mag` has a 0/0 problem at the center.\n\nLooking at my current outward implementation:\n```\nif (rmag > 0.001) {\n  const inv = radialBias / rmag;\n  nu += rx * inv;\n  nv += ry * inv;\n}\n```\n\nThe `rmag > 0.001` guard already handles the singularity (center cell gets no bias). Same guard works for inward \u2014 just flip the sign. So mathematically, this isn't a problem.\n\n**But here's the bigger concern**: inward radial pulls all mass toward the center, and *if edges are open*, no mass leaves. The center cell will accumulate without bound. With current `closed-gravity` semantics where edges are reflected, mass conservation still holds \u2014 the pigment just piles into a small central region.\n\nSo the simulation stays stable (we have VEL_CLAMP, mass conservation, etc.), but **the visual is a tight central pile** rather than the elegant \"everything drains outward\" of the outward radial case.\n\n## What this would look like visually\n\nHonest forecast: inward radial with **open edges** is pointless \u2014 no mass leaves, the open-edge drainage doesn't help, you just get a central pile.\n\nInward radial with **closed-gravity edges** gives you the central pile cleanly: mass piles up at center, no edge buildup.\n\nInward radial with **gravity edges** (open edges in the bias direction) is weird \u2014 the bias points inward but the edges that would open are on the side of the cell's bias direction... which is the side toward center, not toward an edge. Looking at `_recomputeEdgeState` for `gravity` mode: it opens edges where `dx > 0` or `dx < 0` per the bias vector. With radial bias, the per-cell directions vary \u2014 there's no single \"opening\" direction. The current code special-cases 'radial' to open all four edges. For inward radial, opening all edges doesn't make physical sense either (nothing leaves; mass collects at center).\n\nI think the right design is: **inward radial only really makes sense in `closed-gravity` mode**. In `gravity` mode it's degenerate.\n\n## API design\n\nTwo options:\n\n**Option 1 \u2014 A new gravity direction value:** `gravityDirection('radial-in')` or `gravityDirection('inward')`. Composes cleanly with the existing API. Same shape as adding `radial` originally.\n\n**Option 2 \u2014 A separate flag:** `gravityRadialInward(true)` alongside the existing `gravityDirection('radial')`. More verbose but allows future extensions (variable falloff, asymmetric centers, etc.).\n\nOption 1 is the right call. It mirrors the existing `'radial'` value, and the dropdown can grow with `\u2295 Radial (outward)` and `\u2296 Radial (inward)` as separate options. Two strings, parallel structure.\n\nLet me build it.",
    "citations": []
  },

Mirrors the existing\n          <code>'radial'</code> (outward) by flipping the sign:\n          each cell's bias points TOWARD the canvas center instead\n          of away from it. Pigment piles up toward the center over\n          time.\n        </p>\n        <h3>How it differs from outward radial</h3>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><strong>Outward radial:</strong> divergent flow. Mass moves toward edges. With open edges (gravity mode), mass drains off the canvas. Used for \"fade outward\" backgrounds.</li>\n          <li><strong>Inward radial:</strong> convergent flow. Mass moves toward center. Opening edges would be counterproductive (and physically wrong \u2014 pigment wouldn't reach an open edge anyway), so edge mode automatically treats this case as closed regardless of which mode you set. Most useful in <code>closed-gravity</code> mode where mass piles at center without loss.</li>\n        </ul>\n        <h3>Implementation</h3>\n        <p>\n          Shares the per-cell radial-vector math with outward\n          radial, just flips the sign:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">if (_gravityDir === 'radial' || _gravityDir === 'radial-in') {\n  const cx = (GW - 1) * 0.5;\n  const cy = (GH - 1) * 0.5;\n  const rx = x - cx;\n  const ry = y - cy;\n  const rmag = Math.sqrt(rx * rx + ry * ry);\n  if (rmag &gt; 0.001) {\n    const sign = _gravityDir === 'radial-in' ? -1 : 1;\n    const radialBias = sign * _gravityStrength * VEL_CLAMP;\n    const inv = radialBias / rmag;\n    nu += rx * inv;\n    nv += ry * inv;\n  }\n}</pre>\n        <p>\n          The <code>rmag &gt; 0.001</code> guard handles the\n          singularity at canvas center where the displacement vector\n          becomes zero. Same guard as outward radial \u2014 center cells\n          get no bias.\n        </p>\n        <h3>Numerical verification</h3>\n        <p>\n          Painted a 30-cell blob at (150, 150) on a 648\u00d7540 grid\n          (canvas center: (324, 270)) and ran 300 sim steps with\n          gravityStrength 0.15 in closed-gravity mode:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">radial     fraction near center (120-cell disk): 0.0% \u2192 0.0%\nradial-in  fraction near center (120-cell disk): 0.0% \u2192 0.6%</pre>\n        <p>\n          Mass conservation in both cases is ~0.07% loss\n          (evaporation only) \u2014 closed-gravity correctly prevents\n          drainage in both directions.\n        </p>\n        <h3>UI</h3>\n        <p>\n          New <code>\u2296 Radial (inward toward center)</code> option\n          at the bottom of the gravity direction dropdown,\n          alongside the existing <code>\u2295 Radial (outward from\n          center)</code>.\n        </p>\n\n'''\nsrc = src.replace(old_cl, new_cl + old_cl)\nopen('/home/claude/watercolor.html', 'w').write(src)\nprint('docs updated')\nPY\n\nmv /home/claude/watercolor-v0.92-wiring.js /home/claude/watercolor-v0.93-wiring.js\nsed -i 's/v0\\.92-wiring/v0.93-wiring/g' /home/claude/watercolor-v0.93-wiring.js\n\ncd /home/claude && python3 << 'PY'\nsrc = open('/home/claude/watercolor.html').read()\nlines = src.splitlines()\ndef escape(js): return js.replace('</script>', '<\\\\/script>')\nlib = escape(open('/home/claude/washes-pkg/src/washes.js').read())\nwiring = escape(open('/home/claude/watercolor-v0.93-wiring.js').read())\nfor i, line in enumerate(lines):\n    if '<canvas id=\"canvas\"></canvas>' in line:\n        lines[i] = line.replace('<canvas id=\"canvas\"></canvas>', '<div id=\"canvas-host\"></div>')\n        break\nscript_start = None;
