    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-14T08:06:44.469479Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": " This is a much more elegant design than the multi-canvas approach. Let me make sure I understand the architecture and surface the decisions before building.\n\n## What you're describing\n\nA single full-viewport canvas as the visual substrate, with HTML cards positioned *over* it via normal CSS. The cards have real text content (titles, bodies, links \u2014 selectable, accessible, SEO-friendly). The canvas underneath has:\n\n- SVGs stamped at each card's center position\n- Masks drawn over the SVGs (which protect the SVG ink from being washed away)\n- A \"day\" background wash applied to the rest of the canvas\n\nWhen the page loads, this is all set up before anything visible happens. The user sees what looks like cards floating over watercolor-with-embedded-art.\n\nThis is *much* better than four canvases \u2014 fewer simulations to run, no synchronization issues, normal HTML stacking, accessibility just works. The multi-canvas v0.4 exploration was solving a problem that didn't need to exist.\n\n## Decisions worth surfacing before code\n\nA few questions about specifics:\n\n### 1. What's the relationship between cards and the canvas underneath?\n\nTwo possible interpretations:\n\n- **a)** Cards are decorative containers that sit over the canvas at fixed positions. The canvas art under each card is meaningfully tied to that card (this card's icon, this card's mood). When the user moves the cursor near a card, you might do something interactive (deluge under the card, ripple from the card center).\n\n- **b)** Cards are unrelated to the canvas content \u2014 the canvas is just an artistic background that happens to have decorative shapes near where cards happen to be. The cards could move and the canvas art wouldn't follow.\n\nI'll assume **a** since that's why you're stamping SVGs at card positions. Tell me if that's wrong.\n\n### 2. What's the order of operations?\n\nYou wrote:\n1. Canvas dried\n2. Card locations stored\n3. SVGs stamped at card centers\n4. Masks drawn over SVGs\n5. Background \"day\" drawn\n\nI'd want to check the timing carefully because step ordering affects what's visible. Specifically: if step 1 \"dries\" the canvas, it needs to be already wet, which suggests there's an initial wash. Or \"dried\" means \"ensure dry state, no wash applied yet\" \u2014 the canvas is freshly created and empty.\n\nI'll assume **the canvas starts empty, and \"dried\" means \"set evaporation high so any subsequent paint dries fast\"** so the SVGs we stamp stay sharp instead of bleeding. Tell me if you meant something different.\n\n### 3. How are SVGs delivered?\n\nFor each card, do you have:\n- **a)** A different SVG per card (each card has its own icon)\n- **b)** The same SVG with different colors / positions\n- **c)** Something more parametric (the SVG depends on card data)\n\nI'll plan for **a** (each card has its own SVG) since it's the most general. Easy to simplify if **b** or **c** is what you want.\n\n### 4. What's the \"day\" background?\n\nI'll need to define this. Options:\n- A solid warm tone (cream, gold)\n- A linear gradient (light sky to warm horizon)\n- A radial gradient (bright center to dimmer edges)\n- An animated time-of-day wash that slowly changes\n\nThe phrasing \"day\" suggests this could be one of several states (day / night / dawn / dusk). Worth planning for that.\n\nI'll go with **a linear gradient for \"day\"** as the baseline and write the code so other states can be added by changing one config object.\n\n### 5. Card \u2192 canvas coordinate mapping\n\nThis is the actual mechanic that needs the most care. The card is a DOM element at a `getBoundingClientRect()` position; the SVG and mask need to be stamped in canvas coordinates. So before each stamp:\n\n```js\nconst rect = card.getBoundingClientRect();\nconst hostRect = canvasHost.getBoundingClientRect();\nconst cx = rect.left + rect.width / 2 - hostRect.left;\nconst cy = rect.top + rect.height / 2 - hostRect.top;\n// (cx, cy) is now in display pixels relative to the canvas host\n```\n\nAnd then when the user resizes the window, the cards reflow but the canvas pigment stays where it was. **What should happen?** Two options:\n\n- **a)** Re-stamp everything on resize (redraw canvas, re-stamp SVGs at new positions, re-apply masks, re-apply background)\n- **b)** Snap canvas to current state on resize, accept that SVGs are now slightly off-center from cards\n\n**a** is more correct but feels heavy; the user sees a flash. **b** is faster but looks broken if the user resizes a lot.\n\nI'll plan for **a** with a debounce so we only re-stamp after the user finishes resizing (~150ms). Tell me if you want **b** instead.\n\n## What I'm going to build\n\nGiven the assumptions above, the simplest deliverable is:\n\n- A single self-contained HTML file demonstrating the pattern\n- One full-viewport canvas at z-index 0\n- Three cards positioned at varying spots over it, z-index 10, with real text content\n- A setup function that:\n  - Sets initial conditions (high evaporation for fast drying, \"day\" background gradient via `wc.background()`)\n  - Reads each card's bounding rect\n  - Stamps a SVG at each card's center via `wc.traceSVG(svg, { animate: false, bounds: ... })`\n  - Programmatically draws a mask over each SVG so subsequent washes don't disturb it\n  - Applies a \"day\" wash over the rest of the canvas\n- A resize handler that re-runs setup with a debounce\n\nThe cards will be clickable links so accessibility and SEO work normally.\n\nThis is the right scope for a demo. I'll build it as a separate file (so it doesn't disturb the playground), and you can use it as a starting point for your portfolio site.\n\n## Before I code \u2014 three quick checks\n\n1. Confirm cards-are-tied-to-canvas-content (assumption #1) is right\n2. Confirm \"dried\" means high evaporation, canvas starts empty (assumption #2)\n3. Want the demo to be one HTML file you can preview, or scaffolding inside the package's `examples/` folder?\n\nIf you say yes to all three, I'll build it. Anything different, let me know what to change.",

Optional. */\n  .canvas-hint {\n    position: fixed;\n    bottom: 16px;\n    left: 16px;\n    z-index: 20;\n    font-size: 11px;\n    color: rgba(60, 50, 30, 0.5);\n    letter-spacing: 0.02em;\n    pointer-events: none;\n  }\n</style>\n</head>\n<body>\n  <div id=\"canvas-host\"></div>\n\n  <main class=\"cards\">\n    <article class=\"card\" data-svg=\"mark-1\">\n      <h2>Work</h2>\n      <p>\n        Selected projects from the last few years.\n        Watercolor simulations, design systems,\n        the occasional small game.\n      </p>\n      <a href=\"#work\">View projects \u2192</a>\n    </article>\n\n    <article class=\"card\" data-svg=\"mark-2\">\n      <h2>Writing</h2>\n      <p>\n        Notes on design, computer graphics,\n        and the rare joy of building tools\n        for other designers.\n      </p>\n      <a href=\"#writing\">Read essays \u2192</a>\n    </article>\n\n    <article class=\"card\" data-svg=\"mark-3\">\n      <h2>About</h2>\n      <p>\n        Designer, sometimes programmer, currently\n        nerd-sniped at the Recurse Center building\n        watercolor in the browser.\n      </p>\n      <a href=\"#about\">More \u2192</a>\n    </article>\n  </main>\n\n  <div class=\"canvas-hint\">paper is wet \u2014 drag to paint</div>\n\n  <script type=\"module\">\n    // ============================================================\n    // 1. LOAD THE LIB\n    // ============================================================\n    // In a real portfolio site, you'd import this from the npm\n    // package: `import { Washes } from 'washes';`\n    // For this self-contained demo, we load it inline from a CDN-\n    // style path \u2014 but for now I'll have you replace the URL below\n    // with your local washes.js, OR I'll inline it. Since this\n    // demo is meant to be self-contained, I'll inline the lib in\n    // the build step. (See the build script that produces this\n    // file with the lib injected.)\n    //\n    // The build script replaces the marker comment below with the\n    // entire contents of src/washes.js followed by an explicit\n    // assignment to window.Washes (the lib's IIFE already does\n    // this, but being explicit costs nothing and protects us if\n    // the IIFE's globalThis assignment is ever lost).\n    /* {{LIB_INLINE_HERE}} */\n  </script>\n\n  <script type=\"module\">\n    // ============================================================\n    // 2. ABSTRACT SVG MARKS (one per card)\n    // ============================================================\n    // Three abstract organic marks designed for visual balance\n    // when stamped at card centers. Each SVG is viewBox 0 0 200 200\n    // so they render at the same scale relative to the bounds\n    // we pass to traceSVG. No fills (the lib treats stroke as the\n    // pen path). Pigment hex colors (#FF0000 = rose, #FFFF00 =\n    // yellow, #0000FF = blue) trigger the lib's chroma-key\n    // pigment recognition.\n    const MARKS = {\n      // Mark 1: A loose downward arc with a small offset dot \u2014\n      // suggests a calm, intentional first gesture. Rose-toned.\n      'mark-1': `<svg viewBox=\"0 0 200 200\" xmlns=\"http://www.w3.org/2000/svg\">\n        <path d=\"M 30 60 C 50 110, 100 130, 170 100\"\n              fill=\"none\" stroke=\"#FF0000\" stroke-width=\"3\" />\n        <circle cx=\"160\" cy=\"60\" r=\"6\" fill=\"none\" stroke=\"#FF0000\" stroke-width=\"3\" />\n      </svg>`,\n\n      // Mark 2: A spiraling outward curve \u2014 suggests motion and\n      // unfolding. Yellow-toned to feel sun-warm.\n      'mark-2': `<svg viewBox=\"0 0 200 200\" xmlns=\"http://www.w3.org/2000/svg\">\n        <path d=\"M 100 100 C 100 60, 140 60, 140 100 C 140 150, 80 150, 80 100 C 80 40, 160 40, 160 110\"\n              fill=\"none\" stroke=\"#FFFF00\" stroke-width=\"3\" />\n      </svg>`,\n\n      // Mark 3: Two crossing curves \u2014 suggests connection or\n      // intersection. Blue-toned for contrast.\n      'mark-3': `<svg viewBox=\"0 0 200 200\" xmlns=\"http://www.w3.org/2000/svg\">\n        <path d=\"M 40 50 C 80 80, 120 120, 160 150\"\n              fill=\"none\" stroke=\"#0000FF\" stroke-width=\"3\" />\n        <path d=\"M 160 50 C 120 80, 80 120, 40 150\"\n              fill=\"none\" stroke=\"#0000FF\" stroke-width=\"3\" />\n      </svg>`,\n    };\n\n    // ============================================================\n    // 3. CREATE THE CANVAS\n    // ============================================================\n    const host = document.getElementById('canvas-host');\n    const wc = window.Washes.create(host, {\n      // Light starting scale \u2014 fast on most devices\n      qualityHint: 'auto-mobile',\n      // Don't show the brush preview circle for the portfolio\n      // visit \u2014 feels less like a tool and more like a finished\n      // surface. Re-enable if you want the lib's playful brush hint.\n      cursorPreview: false,\n    });\n\n    // ============================================================\n    // 4. SETUP: stamp art under cards, mask, then wash\n    // ============================================================\n    // Idempotent \u2014 safe to call again on resize.\n    async function setup() {\n      // (a) Clear any previous state, dry the canvas. We use a\n      //     high evaporation rate so anything painted in this\n      //     function dries quickly (SVGs stay sharp instead of\n      //     spreading like a wet wash). After setup we'll lower\n      //     it back so user paint behaves naturally.\n      wc.evaporation(50);  // bone-dry while we work\n      wc.removeMask();     // clear any prior mask\n\n      // (b) Background gradient \u2014 the \"day\" backdrop. Composes\n      //     orthogonally with the canvas; pigment renders on top\n      //     of this via the auto-enabled transparent mode.\n      //     Warm cream sky \u2192 soft peach horizon.\n      wc.background('linear-gradient(180deg, #fef3c7 0%, #fde7c8 65%, #f4d3a8 100%)');\n\n      // (c) Stamp the SVG mark under each card. Card positions\n      //     are read live via getBoundingClientRect so resizing\n      //     keeps the art aligned. SVG bounds are sized at ~70%\n      //     of each card's width to leave breathing room.\n      const cards = document.querySelectorAll('.card');\n      const hostRect = host.getBoundingClientRect();\n      const stamps = [];   // [{cx, cy, r}] for masking pass\n\n      for (const card of cards) {\n        const svgKey = card.dataset.svg;\n        const svgText = MARKS[svgKey];\n        if (!svgText) continue;\n\n        const rect = card.getBoundingClientRect();\n        // Card center in display pixels relative to canvas host\n        const cx = rect.left + rect.width / 2 - hostRect.left;\n        const cy = rect.top - hostRect.top + 60;  // upper-mid of card\n        // Mark size: 70% of card width, no larger than 220px\n        const size = Math.min(220, rect.width * 0.7);\n        const half = size / 2;\n\n        // Convert display-pixel bounds to grid coordinates via\n        // toGrid. traceSVG accepts bounds in grid coordinates.\n        const tl = wc.toGrid(cx - half, cy - half);\n        const br = wc.toGrid(cx + half, cy + half);\n        const bounds = {\n          x: tl.gx,\n          y: tl.gy,\n          w: br.gx - tl.gx,\n          h: br.gy - tl.gy,\n        };\n\n        // Stamp the SVG instantly \u2014 no animation, the SVG should\n        // appear whole when the page loads.\n        wc.traceSVG(svgText, {\n          animate: false,\n          bounds: bounds,\n          brushSize: 14,\n          strength: 0.8,\n        });\n\n        // Remember position for the mask pass\n        stamps.push({ cx, cy, r: half * 1.05 });  // mask slightly\n                                                   // beyond SVG bounds\n      }\n\n      // (d) Mask the stamped SVGs so the upcoming wash doesn't\n      //     disturb them. The mask brush freezes cells from any\n      //     subsequent flow \u2014 the SVG ink underneath stays where\n      //     it was put. Mask radius is the same as the SVG bounds.\n      const prevPigment = wc.pigment();\n      wc.pigment('mask');\n      for (const s of stamps) {\n        // Convert display pixels to grid coords\n        const { gx, gy } = wc.toGrid(s.cx, s.cy);\n        const gridR = s.r / wc.scale();\n        // Strength 1.0 for fully-frozen mask\n        wc.paintAt(gx, gy, gridR, 'mask', 1.0);\n      }\n      wc.pigment(prevPigment);\n\n      // (e) Now paint the \"day\" wash over the canvas. This is a\n      //     soft yellow watercolor wash applied broadly so the\n      //     canvas has texture even outside the card regions.\n      //     The mask we just laid down protects the SVG areas.\n      //     We loop a few large yellow stamps across the canvas\n      //     so the wash has organic variation.\n      wc.pigment('yellow');\n      const w = host.clientWidth, h = host.clientHeight;\n      const washPoints = [\n        { x: w * 0.10, y: h * 0.20 },\n        { x: w * 0.45, y: h * 0.85 },\n        { x: w * 0.85, y: h * 0.30 },\n        { x: w * 0.20, y: h * 0.70 },\n        { x: w * 0.70, y: h * 0.60 },\n      ];\n      for (const p of washPoints) {\n        const { gx, gy } = wc.toGrid(p.x, p.y);\n        const gridR = 280 / wc.scale();  // big soft brush\n        wc.paintAt(gx, gy, gridR, 1, 0.10);  // pigment idx 1 = yellow, low strength\n      }\n      // A faint rose wash in the upper-right corner for warmth\n      wc.pigment('rose');\n      const { gx: rgx, gy: rgy } = wc.toGrid(w * 0.95, h * 0.10);\n      wc.paintAt(rgx, rgy, 320 / wc.scale(), 0, 0.05);\n\n      // (f) Restore evaporation so subsequent user interaction\n      //     has natural wet-paint behavior. The wash we just\n      //     painted will dry over the next ~2 seconds with the\n      //     lower evaporation rate, leaving the SVGs sharp and\n      //     the masks holding firm.\n      wc.evaporation(10);\n\n      // (g) Pigment back to a neutral default so the user's\n      //     first brush stroke uses rose.\n      wc.pigment('rose');\n    }\n\n    // ============================================================\n    // 5. RESIZE HANDLING\n    // ============================================================\n    // Re-run setup when the viewport changes size, debounced so\n    // we don't re-stamp on every pixel of a window drag.\n    let resizeTimer = null;\n    window.addEventListener('resize', () => {\n      if (resizeTimer) clearTimeout(resizeTimer);\n      resizeTimer = setTimeout(() => {\n        resizeTimer = null;\n        setup();\n      }, 200);\n    });\n\n    // ============================================================\n    // 6. KICK IT OFF\n    // ============================================================\n    // requestAnimationFrame ensures the cards have laid out and\n    // their getBoundingClientRect is meaningful before we read\n    // positions. Without this, we'd sometimes stamp at (0,0).\n    requestAnimationFrame(() => setup());\n  </script>\n</body>\n</html>\n"
    },

Mask radius is the same as the SVG bounds.\\n      const prevPigment = wc.pigment();\\n      wc.pigment('mask');\\n      for (const s of stamps) {\\n        // Convert display pixels to grid coords\\n        const { gx, gy } = wc.toGrid(s.cx, s.cy);\\n        const gridR = s.r / wc.scale();\\n        // Strength 1.0 for fully-frozen mask\\n        wc.paintAt(gx, gy, gridR, 'mask', 1.0);\\n      }\\n      wc.pigment(prevPigment);\\n\\n      // (e) Now paint the \\\"day\\\" wash over the canvas. RESIZE HANDLING\\n    // ============================================================\\n    // Re-run setup when the viewport changes size, debounced so\\n    // we don't re-stamp on every pixel of a window drag.\\n    let resizeTimer = null;\\n    window.addEventListener('resize', () => {\\n      if (resizeTimer) clearTimeout(resizeTimer);\\n      resizeTimer = setTimeout(() => {\\n        resizeTimer = null;\\n        setup();\\n      }, 200);\\n    });\\n\\n    // ============================================================\\n    // 6. KICK IT OFF\\n    // ============================================================\\n    // requestAnimationFrame ensures the cards have laid out and\\n    // their getBoundingClientRect is meaningful before we read\\n    // positions. Without this, we'd sometimes stamp at (0,0).\\n    requestAnimationFrame(() => setup());\\n  </script>\\n</body>\\n</html>\\n\", \"filename\": \"/home/claude/portfolio-demo.html\"}"

Open it in a browser, see:\n\n- A full-viewport watercolor canvas as the background\n- Three cards (Work / Writing / About) arranged horizontally over it\n- Under each card: an abstract SVG mark (rose arc, yellow spiral, blue crossing curves), stamped at the upper-center of the card position\n- A soft watercolor wash in yellow + faint rose tones across the rest of the canvas\n- Cards are real HTML \u2014 selectable text, hoverable links, accessible to screen readers\n\n**The setup sequence** (everything happens before the first user interaction):\n\n1. Evaporation set to 50 (bone-dry) so SVG stamps stay sharp instead of bleeding\n2. Mask cleared (idempotency for resize)\n3. Background gradient set: cream \u2192 peach (the \"day\" backdrop)\n4. For each card: read its `getBoundingClientRect`, compute canvas coords, stamp the SVG via `traceSVG(svg, { animate: false, bounds })`\n5. Mask painted over each SVG position (radius slightly larger than the SVG bounds) so subsequent washes can't disturb the marks\n6. Yellow wash painted across the canvas at 5 large soft brush positions, plus a small rose accent in the upper-right corner\n7. Evaporation lowered back to 10 (normal wetness) so user interaction feels natural\n8. Active brush set to rose\n\n**Resize handling**: Debounced at 200ms. When the user finishes resizing, `setup()` re-runs and re-stamps everything at the new card positions. This is heavier than option B (snap canvas to current state) but keeps the SVGs aligned with the cards.\n\n## Things worth noticing\n\n**The SVG marks use pigment-hex trigger colors** (`#FF0000` rose, `#FFFF00` yellow, `#0000FF` blue). The lib's `triggerColors` option (on by default in `traceSVG`) recognizes these and routes the strokes to the corresponding pigment. If you want different colors per mark, edit the SVG stroke colors and they'll re-route automatically.\n\n**The cards have `backdrop-filter: blur(8px)`** so text stays readable even where the canvas paint has texture beneath them. The translucency (55% opacity) lets some of the watercolor show through, which is the design intent.\n\n**Cards are positioned via CSS Grid**, not absolute positioning. They reflow naturally on resize, and the canvas re-stamps to match. On mobile (or any narrow viewport), you'd want to switch to a stacked layout \u2014 the Grid template doesn't currently handle that, but it's a one-line change to add a media query.\n\n## Things to try\n\n1. **Drag the canvas** \u2014 paint over the design. The SVGs are masked so your stroke flows around them. (Hint at bottom-left tells the user this.)\n\n2. **Hover the cards** \u2014 they lift slightly. This is pure CSS, no canvas interaction.\n\n3. **Resize the window** \u2014 wait 200ms, the SVGs re-stamp at new card positions. Watch the canvas \"redraw\" itself for the new layout.\n\n4. **Try different \"moods\"**: change the call to `wc.background('linear-gradient(180deg, #fef3c7 0%, #fde7c8 65%, #f4d3a8 100%)')` to:\n   - `'linear-gradient(180deg, #1a1a2e 0%, #16213e 100%)'` for \"night\"\n   - `'linear-gradient(180deg, #fde7c8 0%, #f4a8a8 100%)'` for \"dusk\"\n   - `'radial-gradient(circle at 50% 30%, #fefffb 0%, #d4dde8 100%)'` for \"overcast\"\n\n## What I'd improve if I were polishing this for production\n\n- **Add a \"loading\" state**: 100ms of empty canvas before the SVGs appear isn't ideal. Use `wc.pause()` until setup completes, then `resume()`.\n- **Mobile layout**: stack the cards vertically on narrow screens via a CSS Grid breakpoint.\n- **Card-to-canvas interaction**: hover a card \u2192 small radial deluge under it. This is the \"magical\" version of the demo, where the canvas reacts to cursor proximity. Easy to add \u2014 `mouseenter` handler \u2192 `wc.splash([{x, y, velocity: 15}], 'splash', { radius: 80 })` at the card center.\n\nThe first two are 10-line tweaks; the third is more design work. The canvas underneath gets no pointer events at all.\n\nFix: `pointer-events: none` on `.cards`, then `pointer-events: auto` on `.card` so the cards themselves still work.\n\n## Why you can't see the SVG marks\n\nTwo compounding problems:\n\n1. **The SVG marks are stamped BEHIND the cards** at `card.top + 60`, fully within the card's vertical region.\n2. **The cards have `background: rgba(255, 252, 244, 0.55)` + `backdrop-filter: blur(8px)`** \u2014 meaning the cards are 55% opaque AND blur whatever's behind them. The SVGs get washed out almost completely.\n\nI interpreted \"in the center of the Cards' location\" as \"directly under the cards\" \u2014 but the result is invisible marks, which clearly isn't what you want. Pigment matches the card's SVG color: Work=rose, Writing=yellow, About=blue. Looks intentional \u2014 like the mark is anchoring\n      //     each card from above.\n      const cards = document.querySelectorAll('.card');\n      const hostRect = host.getBoundingClientRect();\n      const stamps = [];   // [{cx, cy, r, pigment}] for masking + interaction\n\n      // Pigment-per-card map for both the SVG triggerColor matching\n      // AND the hover-splash interaction below. Keeping it in one\n      // place ensures both stays in sync if you change the SVG.\n      const PIGMENT_BY_CARD = {\n        'mark-1': 'rose',\n        'mark-2': 'yellow',\n        'mark-3': 'blue',\n      };\n\n      for (const card of cards) {\n        const svgKey = card.dataset.svg;\n        const svgText = MARKS[svgKey];\n        if (!svgText) continue;\n\n        const rect = card.getBoundingClientRect();\n        // Card center-x in display pixels relative to canvas host\n        const cx = rect.left + rect.width / 2 - hostRect.left;\n        // v2 \u2014 vertical position is the TOP EDGE of the card\n        // (was top+60 = behind the card). This puts the SVG's\n        // vertical center at the card's top, making the upper\n        // half visible above the card.\n        const cy = rect.top - hostRect.top;\n        // Mark size: 60% of card width, no larger than 200px.\n        // Slightly smaller than the previous 70% / 220px so the\n        // marks don't dominate the cards visually.\n        const size = Math.min(200, rect.width * 0.6);\n        const half = size / 2;\n\n        // Convert display-pixel bounds to grid coordinates via\n        // toGrid. traceSVG accepts bounds in grid coordinates.\n        const tl = wc.toGrid(cx - half, cy - half);\n        const br = wc.toGrid(cx + half, cy + half);\n        const bounds = {\n          x: tl.gx,\n          y: tl.gy,\n          w: br.gx - tl.gx,\n          h: br.gy - tl.gy,\n        };\n\n        // v2 \u2014 bolder strokes (brushSize 22, strength 0.95) so the\n        // mark reads clearly even where its lower half overlaps\n        // the translucent card. Was brushSize 14, strength 0.8.\n        wc.traceSVG(svgText, {\n          animate: false,\n          bounds: bounds,\n          brushSize: 22,\n          strength: 0.95,\n        });\n\n        // Remember position + pigment for the mask pass AND for\n        // the hover-splash interaction below.\n        stamps.push({\n          card: card,\n          cx: cx,\n          cy: cy,\n          r: half * 1.05,  // mask slightly beyond SVG bounds\n          pigment: PIGMENT_BY_CARD[svgKey] || 'rose',\n        });\n      }",

      "description": "Add card-to-canvas hover interaction",
      "new_str": "      // (g) Pigment back to a neutral default so the user's\n      //     first brush stroke uses rose.\n      wc.pigment('rose');\n\n      // (h) Card-to-canvas interaction. On pointerenter, splash a\n      //     small wash at the card's SVG position. The pigment\n      //     matches the card's mark color. Each card has a\n      //     ~600ms cooldown so rapid hovers don't pile up\n      //     splashes (and so the visual breathes between events\n      //     instead of feeling reactive-in-a-twitchy-way).\n      //\n      //     We re-bind listeners on every setup() call because\n      //     setup() also runs on resize, and the card elements\n      //     are still the same DOM nodes (so we just remove the\n      //     prior listener via the stored reference and add a\n      //     fresh one with updated stamp data).\n      for (const stamp of stamps) {\n        const card = stamp.card;\n        // Remove any previously-attached listener so we don't\n        // accumulate them on each setup() (resize) call.\n        if (card._washHoverHandler) {\n          card.removeEventListener('pointerenter', card._washHoverHandler);\n        }\n        // Per-card cooldown. We store the timestamp on the card\n        // element itself rather than in a Map so we don't need a\n        // closure over a setup-scoped variable.\n        card._lastSplashAt = card._lastSplashAt || 0;\n        const handler = (e) => {\n          const now = performance.now();\n          if (now - card._lastSplashAt < 600) return;  // cooldown\n          card._lastSplashAt = now;\n          // Re-read position at splash time \u2014 the card may have\n          // shifted since setup() last computed it (e.g. mid-\n          // resize). Cheap call.\n          const rect = card.getBoundingClientRect();\n          const hostRect = host.getBoundingClientRect();\n          const cx = rect.left + rect.width / 2 - hostRect.left;\n          const cy = rect.top - hostRect.top;\n          // Switch active pigment to the card's color for the\n          // splash, then restore. The 'splash' style produces a\n          // soft circular spread \u2014 gentler than 'deluge' which\n          // would feel too violent for a UI hover.\n          const prevPigment = wc.pigment();\n          wc.pigment(stamp.pigment);\n          wc.splash(\n            [{ x: cx, y: cy, velocity: 18 }],\n            'splash',\n            { radius: 90, pressure: 0.6 }\n          );\n          wc.pigment(prevPigment);\n        };\n        card.addEventListener('pointerenter', handler);\n        card._washHoverHandler = handler;  // store for next removal\n      }\n    }",

Now you can drag the canvas anywhere outside the cards, and the cards still hover/click normally.\n\n**2. SVG marks now visible.** They were stamped directly under the cards at `card.top + 60`, which put them inside the card region. Cards are 55% opaque with `backdrop-filter: blur(8px)` \u2014 they were washing the marks out almost completely. Also bolder strokes (brushSize 22, strength 0.95 vs the original 14/0.8) so the part behind the card translucency still reads.\n\n**3. Card-to-canvas hover interaction.** On `pointerenter` for each card:\n- Splash at the card's top-center (same position as the SVG mark)\n- Pigment matches the card's mark color: Work=rose, Writing=yellow, About=blue\n- `splash` style (gentler than `deluge`), radius 90, velocity 18 \u2014 a soft outward spread\n- 600ms per-card cooldown so rapid hovers don't pile up\n\nThe previous active pigment is saved and restored, so if you've been painting with blue and hover the rose card, you go back to blue afterward.\n\n**Re-binding safety:** `setup()` re-runs on resize. I store the listener reference on the card element (`card._washHoverHandler`) and remove the old listener before adding a new one. Without this, every resize would accumulate another listener and you'd eventually get N splashes per hover.\n\n## To verify all three are working\n\n1. **Drag anywhere outside the cards** \u2014 should paint.     "text": "Found two bugs. The conversation is hitting all the right notes \u2014 yes, the SVG painting calls `traceSVG` with `animate: false` (which is correct), but here's what's actually going wrong:\n\n## Bug #1 (the SVG mystery)\n\nThe SVGs ARE being painted correctly. The problem is my mask painting is silently broken, so the SVGs aren't being protected, and the yellow wash then bleeds them out.\n\nLook at `paintAt` in the lib (line 2201): `if (pigmentIdx === MASK_INDEX)`. `MASK_INDEX` is the numeric constant `-4`. In the demo I wrote:\n\n```js\nwc.paintAt(gx, gy, gridR, 'mask', 1.0);  // <-- 'mask' string\n```\n\n`'mask' !== -4`, so the mask branch never runs. The call falls through to the regular pigment branch, which uses `pigmentIdx` as an array index \u2014 `g['mask']` is undefined, the loop NaN-poisons cells but doesn't actually deposit mask. Net result: **no mask is laid down at all**.\n\nThen the wash uses giant brushes (280-cell radius at scale 2.5 \u2248 700 display pixels). It deposits wet+pressure over the entire canvas including the SVG positions. The next frame of simulation, the SVG pigment bleeds out via the unmasked wet field. By the time you look, the SVG marks are diluted into the yellow wash.\n\nFix: `wc.pigment('mask')` then read the numeric value back from `wc.pigment()` (the getter returns the resolved numeric index after the string setter). Use that numeric index when calling `paintAt`.\n\n## Bug #2 (the splash error)\n\nMy TypeScript declaration listed `SplashStyle = 'deluge' | 'splash' | 'spray'`. Looking at the actual `SPLASH_PRESETS` in the lib: the real names are `default`, `bigSplash`, `fineSpritz`, `deluge`. `'splash'` and `'spray'` were never valid \u2014 my type was wrong.\n\nFix: use `'default'` in the demo. The mask brush freezes cells from any\n      //     subsequent flow \u2014 the SVG ink underneath stays where\n      //     it was put. Mask radius is slightly larger than the\n      //     SVG bounds so even bleed at the SVG edge is protected.\n      //\n      //     IMPORTANT: paintAt's mask branch checks\n      //     `pigmentIdx === MASK_INDEX` where MASK_INDEX is the\n      //     numeric constant -4. Passing the string 'mask' silently\n      //     falls through to the regular pigment branch (no mask\n      //     is actually deposited). The lib's pigment() setter DOES\n      //     resolve string names to numeric indices, and pigment()\n      //     with no arg returns the current numeric index. So we\n      //     round-trip 'mask' \u2192 setter \u2192 getter to obtain the\n      //     numeric MASK_INDEX without hardcoding it.\n      const prevPigment = wc.pigment();\n      wc.pigment('mask');\n      const maskIdx = wc.pigment();   // numeric MASK_INDEX\n      for (const s of stamps) {\n        const { gx, gy } = wc.toGrid(s.cx, s.cy);\n        const gridR = s.r / wc.scale();\n        wc.paintAt(gx, gy, gridR, maskIdx, 1.0);\n      }\n      wc.pigment(prevPigment);",

    "text": "Shipped the fixes.\n\n## Summary of what was wrong\n\n**The SVG marks were being painted correctly** \u2014 you were right to ask \"is it being mis-placed?\" because the question pointed me at the wrong thing. The painting was fine; the *protection* of the marks was broken.\n\nSpecifically: my mask paint call `wc.paintAt(gx, gy, gridR, 'mask', 1.0)` passed the **string** `'mask'`, but `paintAt`'s mask branch checks `pigmentIdx === MASK_INDEX` where `MASK_INDEX` is the **numeric** constant `-4`. String !== number, so the mask branch never ran. No mask was actually painted. The subsequent yellow wash deposited a huge wet field over the entire canvas, and on the next sim frame the SVG pigment got bled out by the unprotected water+pressure.\n\nFix: use `wc.pigment('mask')` (which resolves strings) then read back the numeric index via `wc.pigment()` getter. No magic numbers; works for any future renames of the mask pigment.\n\n**The splash style** was just a bad TypeScript declaration on my part. The lib's `SPLASH_PRESETS` only includes `default`, `bigSplash`, `fineSpritz`, and `deluge`. My .d.ts had `'splash'` and `'spray'` which never existed. Fixed both the demo (now uses `'default'`) and the .d.ts type definition.\n\n## Bonus: I also tightened the wash\n\nEven though the mask now actually works, I reduced the wash brush from 280-cell radius to 180-cell radius, and dropped strength from 0.10 to 0.08. Also moved the wash positions toward the corners and edges of the viewport, away from the cards. Two reasons:\n\n1. **Defense in depth**: if I ever introduce a similar bug, the wash won't be aggressive enough to completely destroy the SVGs.\n2. **Better composition**: smaller washes leave the center of the canvas cleaner, and the SVG marks above the cards have more visual breathing room.\n\n## What you should see now\n\n1. **Three SVG marks visible at the top of each card** \u2014 a rose arc with a small offset circle (Work), a yellow spiral (Writing), and two crossing blue curves (About). Upper halves clearly above the cards; lower halves emerging from behind the translucent backdrop.\n2. **A soft yellow wash with subtle peach corners** around the canvas. The wash is gentler now.\n3. **Hover any card** \u2192 a soft splash in the card's color at the SVG position. No more error.\n4. **Drag anywhere outside cards** \u2192 still paints normally.\n\nIf the SVG marks are still not visible, the next thing I'd check is whether `traceSVG` is returning 0 (meaning no points were parsed from the SVG). Open browser DevTools console and add temporarily: `console.log('SVG points:', wc.traceSVG(svgText, opts))` to log the return value. Should be a number > 0.",
